AI.tldr: OOWM introduces an object-oriented world modeling framework using UML diagrams and a three-stage training pipeline to overcome limitations of linear Chain-of-Thought prompting for embodied reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard CoT prompting is insufficient for embodied tasks because linear natural language fails to explicitly represent state-space, object hierarchies, and causal dependencies needed for robust robotic planning. Text offers flexibility but lacks the structure required for effective world modeling.
AI.method: Proposes Object-Oriented World Modeling (OOWM) that structures world as explicit symbolic tuple W = ⟨S, T⟩. Uses UML Class Diagrams to ground visual perception into object hierarchies, and Activity Diagrams for executable control flows. Implements three-stage training pipeline combining SFT with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using outcome-based rewards from final plans.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on MRoom-30k benchmark show OOWM significantly outperforms unstructured textual baselines in planning coherence, execution success, and structural fidelity.
AI.conclusion: OOWM establishes a new paradigm for structured embodied reasoning by successfully combining software engineering formalisms with LLMs to create explicit, executable world models for robotic planning.

title: Cross-Validated Cross-Channel Self-Attention and Denoising for Automatic Modulation Classification
summary: This study addresses a key limitation in deep learning Automatic Modulation Classification (AMC) models, which perform well at high signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) but degrade under noisy conditions due to conventional feature extraction suppressing both discriminative structure and interference. The goal was to develop a feature-preserving denoising method that mitigates the loss of modulation class separation. A deep learning AMC model was proposed, incorporating a cross-channel self-attention block to capture dependencies between in-phase and quadrature components, along with dual-path deep residual shrinkage denoising blocks to suppress noise. Experiments using the RML2018.01a dataset employed stratified sampling across 24 modulation types and 26 SNR levels. Results showed that denoising depth strongly influences robustness at low and moderate SNRs. Compared to benchmark models PET-CGDNN, MCLDNN, and DAE, the proposed model achieved notable accuracy improvements across -8 dB to +2 dB SNR, with increases of 3%, 2.3%, and 14%, respectively. Cross-validation confirmed the model's robustness, yielding a mean accuracy of 62.6%, macro precision of 65.8%, macro-recall of 62.6%, and macro-F1 score of 62.9%. The architecture advances interference-aware AMC by formalizing baseband modeling as orthogonal subproblems and introducing cross-channel attention as a generalized complex interaction operator, with ablations confirming the critical role of feature-preserving denoising for robustness at low-to-medium SNR.

AI.tldr: A deep learning AMC model with cross-channel self-attention and dual-path residual shrinkage denoising blocks achieves improved robustness at low-to-medium SNR by preserving discriminative modulation features while suppressing noise.
AI.motivation: Deep learning AMC models perform well at high SNRs but degrade under noisy conditions because conventional feature extraction suppresses both discriminative structure and interference, leading to loss of modulation class separation.
AI.method: Proposed a deep learning AMC model incorporating: 1) cross-channel self-attention block to capture dependencies between in-phase and quadrature components, and 2) dual-path deep residual shrinkage denoising blocks to suppress noise while preserving features. Experiments used RML2018.01a dataset with stratified sampling across 24 modulation types and 26 SNR levels.
AI.result: Denoising depth strongly influences robustness at low/moderate SNRs. Proposed model outperformed PET-CGDNN, MCLDNN, and DAE with accuracy improvements of 3%, 2.3%, and 14% respectively across -8 dB to +2 dB SNR. Cross-validation yielded mean accuracy 62.6%, macro precision 65.8%, macro-recall 62.6%, macro-F1 62.9%.
AI.conclusion: The architecture advances interference-aware AMC by formalizing baseband modeling as orthogonal subproblems and introducing cross-channel attention as generalized complex interaction operator. Feature-preserving denoising is critical for robustness at low-to-medium SNR, as confirmed by ablation studies.

title: OpeFlo: Automated UX Evaluation via Simulated Human Web Interaction with GUI Grounding
summary: Evaluating web usability typically requires time-consuming user studies and expert reviews, which often limits iteration speed during product development, especially for small teams and agile workflows. We present OpenFlo, a user-experience evaluation agent that simulates user behavior on websites and produces standardized usability. Unlike traditional tools that rely on DOM parsing, OpenFlo grounds actions and observations, enabling it to interact with real web pages end-to-end while maintaining a coherent trace of the user journey. Building on Avenir-Web, our system pairs this robust interaction with simulated user behavior profiles and a structured evaluation protocol that integrates the System Usability Scale (SUS), step-wise Single Ease Questions (SEQ), and concurrent Think Aloud. Subsequently, a comprehensive User Experience (UX) report will be generated. We discuss the architecture of OpenFlo and illustrate how its multimodal grounding improves robustness for web-based interaction and UX evaluation scenarios, paving the way for a new era of continuous, scalable, and data-driven usability testing that empowers every developer to build web interfaces that are usable. Code is available at: https://github.com/Onflow-AI/OpenFlo

AI.tldr: OpenFlo is an AI agent that simulates user behavior on websites to automate usability testing, replacing traditional time-consuming methods with scalable, data-driven evaluation.
AI.motivation: Traditional web usability evaluation methods like user studies and expert reviews are time-consuming and slow down iteration in product development, especially for small teams and agile workflows.
AI.method: OpenFlo grounds actions and observations to interact with real web pages end-to-end using multimodal grounding (beyond DOM parsing), simulates user behavior profiles, and uses a structured evaluation protocol with System Usability Scale (SUS), step-wise Single Ease Questions (SEQ), and concurrent Think Aloud.
AI.result: The system generates comprehensive UX reports and demonstrates improved robustness for web-based interaction and UX evaluation scenarios through its multimodal grounding approach.
AI.conclusion: OpenFlo enables continuous, scalable, data-driven usability testing that empowers developers to build more usable web interfaces, representing a new era of automated UX evaluation.

title: When Can You Poison Rewards? A Tight Characterization of Reward Poisoning in Linear MDPs
summary: We study reward poisoning attacks in reinforcement learning (RL), where an adversary manipulates rewards within constrained budgets to force the target RL agent to adopt a policy that aligns with the attacker's objectives. Prior works on reward poisoning mainly focused on sufficient conditions to design a successful attacker, while only a few studies discussed the infeasibility of targeted attacks. This paper provides the first precise necessity and sufficiency characterization of the attackability of a linear MDP under reward poisoning attacks. Our characterization draws a bright line between the vulnerable RL instances, and the intrinsically robust ones which cannot be attacked without large costs even running vanilla non-robust RL algorithms. Our theory extends beyond linear MDPs -- by approximating deep RL environments as linear MDPs, we show that our theoretical framework effectively distinguishes the attackability and efficiently attacks the vulnerable ones, demonstrating both the theoretical and practical significance of our characterization.

AI.tldr: This paper provides the first precise necessity and sufficiency characterization of attackability in linear MDPs under reward poisoning attacks, distinguishing between vulnerable and intrinsically robust RL instances.
AI.motivation: Prior work on reward poisoning attacks focused mainly on sufficient conditions for successful attacks, with limited discussion on infeasibility of targeted attacks. There's a need for a precise theoretical characterization of when RL systems can be attacked under constrained budgets.
AI.method: The authors develop a theoretical framework to characterize attackability in linear Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) under reward poisoning attacks. They establish both necessary and sufficient conditions for successful attacks. They also extend the analysis by approximating deep RL environments as linear MDPs.
AI.result: The framework successfully distinguishes between vulnerable RL instances and intrinsically robust ones that cannot be attacked without large costs. The theoretical characterization works effectively even when applied to deep RL environments approximated as linear MDPs, demonstrating both theoretical and practical significance.
AI.conclusion: This paper provides the first complete characterization of attackability in linear MDPs under reward poisoning attacks, establishing a clear theoretical boundary between attackable and robust RL systems. The framework has practical applications in security evaluation of RL agents.

title: Factorizing formal contexts from closures of necessity operators
summary: Factorizing datasets is an interesting process in a multitude of approaches, but many times it is not possible or efficient the computation of a factorization of the dataset. A method to obtain independent subcontexts of a formal context with Boolean data was proposed in~\cite{dubois:2012}, based on the operators used in possibility theory. In this paper, we will analyze this method and study different properties related to the pairs of sets from which a factorization of a formal context arises. We also inspect how the properties given in the classical case can be extended to the fuzzy framework, which is essential to obtain a mechanism that allows the computation of independent subcontexts of a fuzzy context.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes and extends a method for factorizing formal contexts using possibility theory operators, studying properties of factorization sets and extending these concepts to fuzzy contexts.
AI.motivation: Factorizing datasets is valuable but often computationally challenging. The authors aim to analyze an existing method for obtaining independent subcontexts of Boolean formal contexts and extend it to the fuzzy framework.
AI.method: The paper analyzes a method based on possibility theory operators for factorizing formal contexts with Boolean data. It studies properties related to the pairs of sets from which factorizations arise and investigates how these properties can be extended to fuzzy contexts.
AI.result: The research provides an analysis of factorization properties in formal contexts and demonstrates how to extend these concepts to fuzzy contexts, enabling computation of independent subcontexts in fuzzy settings.
AI.conclusion: The extension of factorization methods from Boolean to fuzzy contexts is essential for developing computational mechanisms to obtain independent subcontexts in fuzzy formal contexts, expanding the applicability of factorization techniques.

title: Graph-RHO: Critical-path-aware Heterogeneous Graph Network for Long-Horizon Flexible Job-Shop Scheduling
summary: Long-horizon Flexible Job-Shop Scheduling~(FJSP) presents a formidable combinatorial challenge due to complex, interdependent decisions spanning extended time horizons. While learning-based Rolling Horizon Optimization~(RHO) has emerged as a promising paradigm to accelerate solving by identifying and fixing invariant operations, its effectiveness is hindered by the structural complexity of FJSP. Existing methods often fail to capture intricate graph-structured dependencies and ignore the asymmetric costs of prediction errors, in which misclassifying critical-path operations is significantly more detrimental than misclassifying non-critical ones. Furthermore, dynamic shifts in predictive confidence during the rolling process make static pruning thresholds inadequate. To address these limitations, we propose Graph-RHO, a novel critical-path-aware graph-based RHO framework. First, we introduce a topology-aware heterogeneous graph network that encodes subproblems as operation-machine graphs with multi-relational edges, leveraging edge-feature-aware message passing to predict operation stability. Second, we incorporate a critical-path-aware mechanism that injects inductive biases during training to distinguish highly sensitive bottleneck operations from robust ones. Third, we devise an adaptive thresholding strategy that dynamically calibrates decision boundaries based on online uncertainty estimation to align model predictions with the solver's search space. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that \mbox{Graph-RHO} establishes a new state of the art in solution quality and computational efficiency. Remarkably, it exhibits exceptional zero-shot generalization, reducing solve time by over 30\% on large-scale instances (2000 operations) while achieving superior solution quality. Our code is available \href{https://github.com/IntelliSensing/Graph-RHO}{here}.

AI.tldr: Graph-RHO is a novel critical-path-aware graph-based Rolling Horizon Optimization framework that addresses structural complexity in long-horizon Flexible Job-Shop Scheduling, achieving state-of-the-art performance with exceptional zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon FJSP is a formidable combinatorial challenge with complex interdependent decisions. Existing learning-based RHO methods fail to capture intricate graph-structured dependencies, ignore asymmetric costs of prediction errors (where misclassifying critical-path operations is worse), and use static pruning thresholds that are inadequate for dynamic confidence shifts during rolling optimization.
AI.method: Proposes Graph-RHO with three key innovations: 1) A topology-aware heterogeneous graph network encoding subproblems as operation-machine graphs with multi-relational edges, using edge-feature-aware message passing to predict operation stability; 2) A critical-path-aware mechanism injecting inductive biases to distinguish bottleneck operations; 3) An adaptive thresholding strategy dynamically calibrating decision boundaries based on online uncertainty estimation.
AI.result: Establishes new state-of-the-art in solution quality and computational efficiency on standard benchmarks. Exhibits exceptional zero-shot generalization, reducing solve time by over 30% on large-scale instances (2000 operations) while achieving superior solution quality.
AI.conclusion: Graph-RHO successfully addresses the limitations of existing methods by better capturing structural dependencies, handling asymmetric error costs, and adapting to dynamic confidence changes, making it a highly effective approach for complex FJSP problems with strong generalization capabilities.

title: Agentic Exploration of PDE Spaces using Latent Foundation Models for Parameterized Simulations
summary: Flow physics and more broadly physical phenomena governed by partial differential equations (PDEs), are inherently continuous, high-dimensional and often chaotic in nature. Traditionally, researchers have explored these rich spatiotemporal PDE solution spaces using laboratory experiments and/or computationally expensive numerical simulations. This severely limits automated and large-scale exploration, unlike domains such as drug discovery or materials science, where discrete, tokenizable representations naturally interface with large language models. We address this by coupling multi-agent LLMs with latent foundation models (LFMs), a generative model over parametrised simulations, that learns explicit, compact and disentangled latent representations of flow fields, enabling continuous exploration across governing PDE parameters and boundary conditions. The LFM serves as an on-demand surrogate simulator, allowing agents to query arbitrary parameter configurations at negligible cost. A hierarchical agent architecture orchestrates exploration through a closed loop of hypothesis, experimentation, analysis and verification, with a tool-modular interface requiring no user support. Applied to flow past tandem cylinders at Re = 500, the framework autonomously evaluates over 1,600 parameter-location pairs and discovers divergent scaling laws: a regime-dependent two-mode structure for minimum displacement thickness and a robust linear scaling for maximum momentum thickness, with both landscapes exhibiting a dual-extrema structure that emerges at the near-wake to co-shedding regime transition. The coupling of the learned physical representations with agentic reasoning establishes a general paradigm for automated scientific discovery in PDE-governed systems.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent LLMs coupled with latent foundation models enable automated exploration of PDE-governed physical systems, discovering novel scaling laws in fluid flows through autonomous hypothesis testing.
AI.motivation: Traditional methods for exploring PDE-governed physical phenomena (like fluid flows) rely on expensive experiments or simulations, limiting large-scale automated exploration unlike discrete domains like drug discovery. There's a need to bridge this gap between continuous physical systems and AI-driven discovery.
AI.method: Coupling multi-agent LLMs with latent foundation models (LFMs) that learn compact, disentangled latent representations of flow fields. LFMs serve as on-demand surrogate simulators. A hierarchical agent architecture orchestrates exploration through hypothesis-experimentation-analysis-verification loops with tool-modular interface requiring no user support.
AI.result: Applied to flow past tandem cylinders at Re=500, the framework autonomously evaluated over 1,600 parameter-location pairs and discovered divergent scaling laws: regime-dependent two-mode structure for minimum displacement thickness and robust linear scaling for maximum momentum thickness, with dual-extrema structure emerging at near-wake to co-shedding regime transition.
AI.conclusion: The coupling of learned physical representations with agentic reasoning establishes a general paradigm for automated scientific discovery in PDE-governed systems, enabling continuous exploration across governing parameters and boundary conditions at negligible cost.

title: Transformers Learn the Optimal DDPM Denoiser for Multi-Token GMMs
summary: Transformer-based diffusion models have demonstrated remarkable performance at generating high-quality samples. However, our theoretical understanding of the reasons for this success remains limited. For instance, existing models are typically trained by minimizing a denoising objective, which is equivalent to fitting the score function of the training data. However, we do not know why transformer-based models can match the score function for denoising, or why gradient-based methods converge to the optimal denoising model despite the non-convex loss landscape. To the best of our knowledge, this paper provides the first convergence analysis for training transformer-based diffusion models. More specifically, we consider the population Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) objective for denoising data that follow a multi-token Gaussian mixture distribution. We theoretically quantify the required number of tokens per data point and training iterations for the global convergence towards the Bayes optimal risk of the denoising objective, thereby achieving a desired score matching error. A deeper investigation reveals that the self-attention module of the trained transformer implements a mean denoising mechanism that enables the trained model to approximate the oracle Minimum Mean Squared Error (MMSE) estimator of the injected noise in the diffusion steps. Numerical experiments validate these findings.

AI.tldr: First theoretical convergence analysis for training transformer-based diffusion models on multi-token Gaussian mixture data, showing convergence to optimal denoising performance.
AI.motivation: Despite transformer-based diffusion models showing remarkable performance in generating high-quality samples, there's limited theoretical understanding of why they work so well. Specifically, it's unclear why transformer models can match the score function for denoising and why gradient-based methods converge to optimal denoising models despite non-convex loss landscapes.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of the population DDPM objective for denoising data following multi-token Gaussian mixture distributions. Quantifies required number of tokens per data point and training iterations for global convergence to Bayes optimal risk. Investigates how self-attention modules implement mean denoising mechanisms.
AI.result: Provides first convergence analysis showing transformer-based diffusion models can achieve desired score matching error and approximate the oracle MMSE estimator of injected noise. Self-attention modules are shown to implement mean denoising mechanisms that enable optimal performance.
AI.conclusion: This work provides crucial theoretical foundations for understanding why transformer-based diffusion models work, establishing convergence guarantees and revealing the mechanisms through which self-attention enables optimal denoising performance, with numerical experiments validating the theoretical findings.

title: MobiFlow: Real-World Mobile Agent Benchmarking through Trajectory Fusion
summary: Mobile agents can autonomously complete user-assigned tasks through GUI interactions. However, existing mainstream evaluation benchmarks, such as AndroidWorld, operate by connecting to a system-level Android emulator and provide evaluation signals based on the state of system resources. In real-world mobile-agent scenarios, however, many third-party applications do not expose system-level APIs to determine whether a task has succeeded, leading to a mismatch between benchmarks and real-world usage and making it difficult to evaluate model performance accurately. To address these issues, we propose MobiFlow, an evaluation framework built on tasks drawn from arbitrary third-party applications. Using an efficient graph-construction algorithm based on multi-trajectory fusion, MobiFlow can effectively compress the state space, support dynamic interaction, and better align with real-world third-party application scenarios. MobiFlow covers 20 widely used third-party applications and comprises 240 diverse real-world tasks, with enriched evaluation metrics. Compared with AndroidWorld, MobiFlow's evaluation results show higher alignment with human assessments and can guide the training of future GUI-based models under real workloads.

AI.tldr: MobiFlow is an evaluation framework for mobile GUI agents that better aligns with real-world third-party applications by using visual state tracking instead of system-level APIs, addressing limitations of existing benchmarks like AndroidWorld.
AI.motivation: Existing mobile agent benchmarks like AndroidWorld rely on system-level emulators and APIs, which creates a mismatch with real-world scenarios where third-party apps don't expose system-level APIs for task success determination, making accurate model evaluation difficult.
AI.method: Proposes MobiFlow framework built on tasks from arbitrary third-party apps, using an efficient graph-construction algorithm based on multi-trajectory fusion to compress state space, support dynamic interaction, and better align with real-world app scenarios.
AI.result: MobiFlow covers 20 widely used third-party applications with 240 diverse real-world tasks and enriched evaluation metrics. Evaluation results show higher alignment with human assessments compared to AndroidWorld and can guide training of future GUI-based models.
AI.conclusion: MobiFlow addresses the benchmark-reality gap for mobile agent evaluation by providing a more realistic framework for third-party applications, offering better alignment with human judgment and practical guidance for model development under real workloads.

title: Attention Sink in Transformers: A Survey on Utilization, Interpretation, and Mitigation
summary: As the foundational architecture of modern machine learning, Transformers have driven remarkable progress across diverse AI domains. Despite their transformative impact, a persistent challenge across various Transformers is Attention Sink (AS), in which a disproportionate amount of attention is focused on a small subset of specific yet uninformative tokens. AS complicates interpretability, significantly affecting the training and inference dynamics, and exacerbates issues such as hallucinations. In recent years, substantial research has been dedicated to understanding and harnessing AS. However, a comprehensive survey that systematically consolidates AS-related research and offers guidance for future advancements remains lacking. To address this gap, we present the first survey on AS, structured around three key dimensions that define the current research landscape: Fundamental Utilization, Mechanistic Interpretation, and Strategic Mitigation. Our work provides a pivotal contribution by clarifying key concepts and guiding researchers through the evolution and trends of the field. We envision this survey as a definitive resource, empowering researchers and practitioners to effectively manage AS within the current Transformer paradigm, while simultaneously inspiring innovative advancements for the next generation of Transformers. The paper list of this work is available at https://github.com/ZunhaiSu/Awesome-Attention-Sink.

AI.tldr: First comprehensive survey on Attention Sink in Transformers, covering fundamental utilization, mechanistic interpretation, and strategic mitigation approaches.
AI.motivation: Despite Attention Sink being a persistent challenge that complicates interpretability, affects training/inference dynamics, and exacerbates hallucinations, there's no comprehensive survey consolidating AS-related research and guiding future advancements.
AI.method: Structured literature review organized around three key dimensions: Fundamental Utilization (how AS is used), Mechanistic Interpretation (understanding why AS occurs), and Strategic Mitigation (methods to address AS issues).
AI.result: Created the first comprehensive survey on Attention Sink, clarifying key concepts, tracking field evolution/trends, and providing a valuable resource at https://github.com/ZunhaiSu/Awesome-Attention-Sink.
AI.conclusion: This survey serves as a definitive resource to help researchers manage AS in current Transformers while inspiring innovations for next-generation architectures.

title: Persistent Identity in AI Agents: A Multi-Anchor Architecture for Resilient Memory and Continuity
comment: 18 pages, 2 figures. Submitting to arXiv cs.ET (Emerging Technologies)
summary: Modern AI agents suffer from a fundamental identity problem: when context windows overflow and conversation histories are summarized, agents experience catastrophic forgetting -- losing not just information, but continuity of self. This technical limitation reflects a deeper architectural flaw: AI agent identity is centralized in a single memory store, creating a single point of failure. Drawing on neurological case studies of human memory disorders, we observe that human identity survives damage because it is distributed across multiple systems: episodic memory, procedural memory, emotional continuity, and embodied knowledge. We present soul.py, an open-source architecture that implements persistent identity through separable components (identity files and memory logs), and propose extensions toward multi-anchor resilience. The framework introduces a hybrid RAG+RLM retrieval system that automatically routes queries to appropriate memory access patterns, achieving efficient retrieval without sacrificing comprehensiveness. We formalize the notion of identity anchors for AI systems and present a roadmap for building agents whose identity can survive partial memory failures. Code is available at github.com/menonpg/soul.py

AI.tldr: The paper proposes soul.py, an open-source architecture that addresses AI agent identity issues caused by catastrophic forgetting in long conversations by distributing identity across multiple memory systems, inspired by human neurological resilience.
AI.motivation: AI agents suffer from catastrophic forgetting when context windows overflow, leading to loss of self-identity. Current architectures centralize identity in single memory stores, creating single points of failure, unlike human identity which survives memory damage through distributed systems.
AI.method: Soul.py implements persistent identity through separable components (identity files and memory logs) with a hybrid RAG+RLM retrieval system that automatically routes queries to appropriate memory access patterns. The framework formalizes identity anchors for multi-anchor resilience.
AI.result: The framework achieves efficient retrieval without sacrificing comprehensiveness by distributing identity across multiple components, enabling AI agents to maintain continuity of self even during partial memory failures.
AI.conclusion: By distributing AI agent identity across multiple systems like episodic memory, procedural memory, emotional continuity, and embodied knowledge, the soul.py architecture provides a roadmap for building agents whose identity can survive memory failures, similar to human neurological resilience.

title: End-to-end Automated Deep Neural Network Optimization for PPG-based Blood Pressure Estimation on Wearables
summary: Photoplethysmography (PPG)-based blood pressure (BP) estimation is a challenging task, particularly on resource-constrained wearable devices. However, fully on-board processing is desirable to ensure user data confidentiality. Recent deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved high BP estimation accuracy by reconstructing BP waveforms or directly regressing BP values, but their large memory, computation, and energy requirements hinder deployment on wearables. This work introduces a fully automated DNN design pipeline that combines hardware-aware neural architecture search (NAS), pruning, and mixed-precision search (MPS) to generate accurate yet compact BP prediction models optimized for ultra-low-power multicore systems-on-chip (SoCs). Starting from state-of-the-art baseline models on four public datasets, our optimized networks achieve up to 7.99% lower error with a 7.5x parameter reduction, or up to 83x fewer parameters with negligible accuracy loss. All models fit within 512 kB of memory on our target SoC (GreenWaves' GAP8), requiring less than 55 kB and achieving an average inference latency of 142 ms and energy consumption of 7.25 mJ. Patient-specific fine-tuning further improves accuracy by up to 64%, enabling fully autonomous, low-cost BP monitoring on wearables.

AI.tldr: A fully automated DNN design pipeline combining hardware-aware NAS, pruning, and mixed-precision search to generate compact, accurate blood pressure prediction models optimized for wearable devices.
AI.motivation: PPG-based blood pressure estimation is challenging on resource-constrained wearables. While on-board processing is needed for data confidentiality, existing DNNs have excessive memory, computation, and energy requirements that prevent deployment on wearable devices.
AI.method: Developed a fully automated DNN design pipeline combining hardware-aware neural architecture search (NAS), pruning, and mixed-precision search (MPS). The pipeline optimizes models for ultra-low-power multicore SoCs (specifically GreenWaves' GAP8), starting from state-of-the-art baselines on four public datasets.
AI.result: Optimized models achieve up to 7.99% lower error with 7.5x parameter reduction, or up to 83x fewer parameters with negligible accuracy loss. All models fit within 512 kB memory on target SoC, requiring less than 55 kB, with average inference latency of 142 ms and energy consumption of 7.25 mJ. Patient-specific fine-tuning further improves accuracy by up to 64%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed automated DNN design pipeline enables accurate, compact blood pressure prediction models suitable for resource-constrained wearable devices, making fully autonomous, low-cost BP monitoring feasible through on-board processing while maintaining data confidentiality.

title: DeepReviewer 2.0: A Traceable Agentic System for Auditable Scientific Peer Review
summary: Automated peer review is often framed as generating fluent critique, yet reviewers and area chairs need judgments they can \emph{audit}: where a concern applies, what evidence supports it, and what concrete follow-up is required. DeepReviewer~2.0 is a process-controlled agentic review system built around an output contract: it produces a \textbf{traceable review package} with anchored annotations, localized evidence, and executable follow-up actions, and it exports only after meeting minimum traceability and coverage budgets. Concretely, it first builds a manuscript-only claim--evidence--risk ledger and verification agenda, then performs agenda-driven retrieval and writes anchored critiques under an export gate. On 134 ICLR~2025 submissions under three fixed protocols, an \emph{un-finetuned 196B} model running DeepReviewer~2.0 outperforms Gemini-3.1-Pro-preview, improving strict major-issue coverage (37.26\% vs.\ 23.57\%) and winning 71.63\% of micro-averaged blind comparisons against a human review committee, while ranking first among automatic systems in our pool. We position DeepReviewer~2.0 as an assistive tool rather than a decision proxy, and note remaining gaps such as ethics-sensitive checks.

AI.tldr: DeepReviewer 2.0 is a process-controlled agentic review system that produces traceable, auditable review packages with anchored annotations and executable follow-up actions, significantly outperforming strong baselines in automated peer review.
AI.motivation: Current automated peer review systems focus on generating fluent critique but lack auditability - reviewers and area chairs need to understand where concerns apply, what evidence supports them, and what concrete follow-up is required.
AI.method: DeepReviewer 2.0 uses a process-controlled approach with output contract: builds claim-evidence-risk ledger and verification agenda from manuscript, performs agenda-driven retrieval, writes anchored critiques under export gate that requires meeting minimum traceability and coverage budgets.
AI.result: On 134 ICLR 2025 submissions, an un-finetuned 196B model running DeepReviewer 2.0 outperforms Gemini-3.1-Pro-preview (37.26% vs 23.57% strict major-issue coverage) and wins 71.63% of micro-averaged blind comparisons against human review committees, ranking first among automatic systems.
AI.conclusion: The system is positioned as an assistive tool rather than decision proxy, with remaining gaps including ethics-sensitive checks, but demonstrates strong performance in producing traceable, auditable reviews.

title: Consensus-based Recursive Multi-Output Gaussian Process
comment: Submitted to International Workshop on Signal Processing and Artificial Intelligence in Wireless Communications (IEEE SPAWC 2026)
summary: Multi-output Gaussian Processes provide principled uncertainty-aware learning of vector-valued fields but are difficult to deploy in large-scale, distributed, and streaming settings due to their computational and centralized nature. This paper proposes a Consensus-based Recursive Multi-Output Gaussian Process (CRMGP) framework that combines recursive inference on shared basis vectors with neighbour-to-neighbour information-consensus updates. The resulting method supports parallel, fully distributed learning with bounded per-step computation while preserving inter-output correlations and calibrated uncertainty. Experiments on synthetic wind fields and real LiDAR data demonstrate that CRMGP achieves competitive predictive performance and reliable uncertainty calibration, offering a scalable alternative to centralized Gaussian process models for multi-agent sensing applications.

AI.tldr: A distributed, scalable Gaussian Process framework called CRMGP that enables multi-agent sensing with uncertainty calibration while being computationally efficient.
AI.motivation: Multi-output Gaussian Processes are difficult to deploy in large-scale, distributed, and streaming settings due to their computational complexity and centralized nature, creating a need for scalable alternatives for multi-agent sensing applications.
AI.method: Consensus-based Recursive Multi-Output Gaussian Process (CRMGP) framework that combines recursive inference on shared basis vectors with neighbor-to-neighbor information-consensus updates, enabling parallel distributed learning with bounded computation.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic wind fields and real LiDAR data show competitive predictive performance and reliable uncertainty calibration compared to centralized Gaussian process models.
AI.conclusion: CRMGP provides a scalable, distributed alternative to centralized multi-output Gaussian Process models that preserves inter-output correlations and uncertainty calibration while being deployable in real-world multi-agent sensing scenarios.

title: Spatial Competence Benchmark
comment: Accepted at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Efficient Spatial Reasoning
summary: Spatial competence is the quality of maintaining a consistent internal representation of an environment and using it to infer discrete structure and plan actions under constraints. Prevailing spatial evaluations for large models are limited to probing isolated primitives through 3D transformations or visual question answering. We introduce the Spatial Competence Benchmark (SCBench), spanning three hierarchical capability buckets whose tasks require executable outputs verified by deterministic checkers or simulator-based evaluators. On SCBench, three frontier models exhibit monotonically decreasing accuracy up the capability ladder. Sweeping output-token caps shows that accuracy gains concentrate at low budgets and saturate quickly, and failures are dominated by locally plausible geometry that breaks global constraints. We release the task generators, verifiers, and visualisation tooling.

AI.tldr: SCBench is a new spatial reasoning benchmark with three hierarchical capability levels that tests executable outputs, revealing frontier models' declining performance as tasks get harder and showing limited gains from longer responses.
AI.motivation: Existing spatial evaluations for large models are limited to isolated primitives (3D transformations, visual QA), lacking comprehensive assessment of spatial competence - the ability to maintain consistent internal representations and use them for inference and planning under constraints.
AI.method: Introduces SCBench with three hierarchical capability buckets requiring executable outputs verified by deterministic checkers or simulator-based evaluators. Tests models with sweeping output-token caps to analyze response patterns and failure modes.
AI.result: Three frontier models show monotonically decreasing accuracy across capability levels. Accuracy gains concentrate at low token budgets and saturate quickly. Failures dominated by locally plausible geometry that breaks global constraints.
AI.conclusion: SCBench reveals significant gaps in current models' spatial competence, especially at higher reasoning levels. The benchmark with task generators, verifiers, and visualization tools provides a foundation for systematic spatial intelligence evaluation.

title: A Temporally Augmented Graph Attention Network for Affordance Classification
comment: 6 pages, 6 figures. Accepted at 3rd IEEE Guwahati Subsection Conference (GCON 2026)
summary: Graph attention networks (GATs) provide one of the best frameworks for learning node representations in relational data; but, existing variants such as Graph Attention Network (GAT) mainly operate on static graphs and rely on implicit temporal aggregation when applied to sequential data. In this paper, we introduce Electroencephalography-temporal Graph Attention Network (EEG-tGAT), a temporally augmented formulation of GATv2 that is tailored for affordance classification from interaction sequences. The proposed model incorporates temporal attention to modulate the contribution of different time segments and temporal dropout to regularize learning across temporally correlated observations. The design reflects the assumption that temporal dimensions in affordance data are not semantically uniform and that discriminative information may be unevenly distributed across time. Experimental results on affordance datasets show that EEG-tGAT achieves improved classification performance compared to GATv2. The observed gains helps to conclude that explicitly encoding temporal importance and enforcing temporal robustness introduce inductive biases that are much better aligned with the structure of affordance-driven interaction data. These findings show us that modest architectural changes to graph attention models can help one obtain consistent benefits when temporal relationships play a nontrivial role in the task.

AI.tldr: EEG-tGAT extends GATv2 with temporal attention and dropout for better affordance classification from sequential interaction data.
AI.motivation: Existing graph attention networks (GATs) mainly operate on static graphs and rely on implicit temporal aggregation for sequential data, which is insufficient for affordance classification where temporal dimensions are not semantically uniform and discriminative information may be unevenly distributed across time.
AI.method: Proposes EEG-temporal Graph Attention Network (EEG-tGAT), a temporally augmented formulation of GATv2 that incorporates temporal attention to modulate contributions of different time segments and temporal dropout to regularize learning across temporally correlated observations.
AI.result: EEG-tGAT achieves improved classification performance compared to GATv2 on affordance datasets, with observed gains indicating that explicit temporal encoding introduces beneficial inductive biases for affordance-driven interaction data.
AI.conclusion: Modest architectural changes to graph attention models that explicitly encode temporal importance and enforce temporal robustness provide consistent benefits when temporal relationships play a nontrivial role in the task, as shown in affordance classification applications.

title: Tracing the Thought of a Grandmaster-level Chess-Playing Transformer
summary: While modern transformer neural networks achieve grandmaster-level performance in chess and other reasoning tasks, their internal computation process remains largely opaque. Focusing on Leela Chess Zero (LC0), we introduce a sparse decomposition framework to interpret its internal computation by decomposing its MLP and attention modules with sparse replacement layers, which capture the primary computation process of LC0. We conduct a detailed case study showing that these pathways expose rich, interpretable tactical considerations that are empirically verifiable. We further introduce three quantitative metrics and show that LC0 exhibits parallel reasoning behavior consistent with the inductive bias of its policy head architecture. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to decompose the internal computation of a transformer on both MLP and attention modules for interpretability. Combining sparse replacement layers and causal interventions in LC0 provides a comprehensive understanding of advanced tactical reasoning, offering critical insights into the underlying mechanisms of superhuman systems. Our code is available at https://github.com/JacklE0niden/Leela-SAEs.

AI.tldr: Researchers develop a sparse decomposition framework to interpret transformer neural networks' internal computation in chess-playing AI, using Leela Chess Zero as a case study to reveal interpretable tactical reasoning pathways.
AI.motivation: Modern transformer neural networks achieve superhuman performance in chess and reasoning tasks, but their internal computation processes remain largely opaque and uninterpretable, creating a "black box" problem.
AI.method: Introduced sparse decomposition framework using sparse replacement layers to interpret LC0's MLP and attention modules. Combined with causal interventions and three quantitative metrics to analyze parallel reasoning behavior consistent with policy head architecture.
AI.result: The framework successfully exposes rich, interpretable tactical considerations that are empirically verifiable. Shows LC0 exhibits parallel reasoning behavior aligned with its policy head architecture's inductive bias.
AI.conclusion: This is the first work to decompose transformer computation on both MLP and attention modules for interpretability. Combining sparse replacement layers with causal interventions provides comprehensive understanding of advanced tactical reasoning in superhuman AI systems.

title: CID-TKG: Collaborative Historical Invariance and Evolutionary Dynamics Learning for Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning
summary: Temporal knowledge graph (TKG) reasoning aims to infer future facts at unseen timestamps from temporally evolving entities and relations. Despite recent progress, existing approaches still suffer from inherent limitations due to their inductive biases, as they predominantly rely on time-invariant or weakly time-dependent structures and overlook the evolutionary dynamics. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel collaborative learning framework for TKGR (dubbed CID-TKG) that integrates evolutionary dynamics and historical invariance semantics as an effective inductive bias for reasoning. Specifically, CID-TKG constructs a historical invariance graph to capture long-term structural regularities and an evolutionary dynamics graph to model short-term temporal transitions. Dedicated encoders are then employed to learn representations from each structure. To alleviate semantic discrepancies across the two structures, we decompose relations into view-specific representations and align view-specific query representations via a contrastive objective, which promotes cross-view consistency while suppressing view-specific noise. Extensive experiments verify that our CID-TKG achieves state-of-the-art performance under extrapolation settings.

AI.tldr: CID-TKG: A collaborative learning framework for temporal knowledge graph reasoning that integrates evolutionary dynamics and historical invariance semantics to capture both short-term transitions and long-term structural regularities.
AI.motivation: Existing TKG reasoning approaches suffer from limitations due to their inductive biases - they rely on time-invariant or weakly time-dependent structures and overlook evolutionary dynamics, which hinders accurate future fact inference from temporally evolving entities and relations.
AI.method: Proposes CID-TKG framework that constructs: 1) Historical invariance graph to capture long-term structural regularities, 2) Evolutionary dynamics graph to model short-term temporal transitions. Uses dedicated encoders for each structure, decomposes relations into view-specific representations, and aligns query representations via contrastive objective to alleviate semantic discrepancies and promote cross-view consistency while suppressing view-specific noise.
AI.result: Extensive experiments verify that CID-TKG achieves state-of-the-art performance under extrapolation settings for temporal knowledge graph reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed collaborative learning framework effectively integrates evolutionary dynamics and historical invariance semantics as inductive bias for TKG reasoning, overcoming limitations of existing approaches and achieving superior performance in predicting future facts at unseen timestamps.

title: Virtual Smart Metering in District Heating Networks via Heterogeneous Spatial-Temporal Graph Neural Networks
summary: Intelligent operation of thermal energy networks aims to improve energy efficiency, reliability, and operational flexibility through data-driven control, predictive optimization, and early fault detection. Achieving these goals relies on sufficient observability, requiring continuous and well-distributed monitoring of thermal and hydraulic states. However, district heating systems are typically sparsely instrumented and frequently affected by sensor faults, limiting monitoring. Virtual sensing offers a cost-effective means to enhance observability, yet its development and validation remain limited in practice. Existing data-driven methods generally assume dense synchronized data, while analytical models rely on simplified hydraulic and thermal assumptions that may not adequately capture the behavior of heterogeneous network topologies. Consequently, modeling the coupled nonlinear dependencies between pressure, flow, and temperature under realistic operating conditions remains challenging. In addition, the lack of publicly available benchmark datasets hinders systematic comparison of virtual sensing approaches. To address these challenges, we propose a heterogeneous spatial-temporal graph neural network (HSTGNN) for constructing virtual smart heat meters. The model incorporates the functional relationships inherent in district heating networks and employs dedicated branches to learn graph structures and temporal dynamics for flow, temperature, and pressure measurements, thereby enabling the joint modeling of cross-variable and spatial correlations. To support further research, we introduce a controlled laboratory dataset collected at the Aalborg Smart Water Infrastructure Laboratory, providing synchronized high-resolution measurements representative of real operating conditions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach significantly outperforms existing baselines.

AI.tldr: HSTGNN for virtual smart heat meters in district heating systems outperforms baselines using a new lab dataset for validation.
AI.motivation: District heating systems suffer from sparse instrumentation, sensor faults, and lack of observability. Existing methods either need dense synchronized data or rely on oversimplified analytical models, while benchmark datasets for systematic comparison are lacking.
AI.method: Heterogeneous Spatial-Temporal Graph Neural Network (HSTGNN) with dedicated branches to learn graph structures and temporal dynamics for flow, temperature, and pressure measurements, enabling joint modeling of cross-variable and spatial correlations.
AI.result: Proposed HSTGNN significantly outperforms existing baselines. Additionally introduced a controlled laboratory dataset from Aalborg Smart Water Infrastructure Laboratory with synchronized high-resolution measurements.
AI.conclusion: The HSTGNN approach effectively addresses the challenges of virtual sensing in district heating by modeling coupled nonlinear dependencies and cross-variable correlations, while the provided dataset enables systematic comparison of virtual sensing methods.

title: Hubble: An LLM-Driven Agentic Framework for Safe and Automated Alpha Factor Discovery
summary: Discovering predictive alpha factors in quantitative finance remains a formidable challenge due to the vast combinatorial search space and inherently low signal-to-noise ratios in financial data. Existing automated methods, particularly genetic programming, often produce complex, uninterpretable formulas prone to overfitting. We introduce Hubble, a closed-loop factor mining framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) as intelligent search heuristics, constrained by a domain-specific operator language and an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)-based execution sandbox. The framework evaluates candidate factors through a rigorous statistical pipeline encompassing cross-sectional Rank Information Coefficient (RankIC), annualized Information Ratio, and portfolio turnover. An evolutionary feedback mechanism returns top-performing factors and structured error diagnostics to the LLM, enabling iterative refinement across multiple generation rounds. In experiments conducted on a panel of 30 U.S. equities over 752 trading days, the system evaluated 181 syntactically valid factors from 122 unique candidates across three rounds, achieving a peak composite score of 0.827 with 100% computational stability. Our results demonstrate that combining LLM-driven generation with deterministic safety constraints yields an effective, interpretable, and reproducible approach to automated factor discovery.

AI.tldr: Hubble is an automated factor discovery framework that uses LLMs as intelligent search heuristics within safety constraints to generate predictive financial factors, achieving both effectiveness and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Automated discovery of predictive alpha factors in finance is challenging due to vast search spaces and low signal-to-noise ratios. Existing methods like genetic programming produce complex, uninterpretable formulas prone to overfitting.
AI.method: Hubble uses LLMs as intelligent search heuristics within a constrained domain-specific operator language and AST-based execution sandbox. It employs an evolutionary feedback loop where candidate factors are evaluated using statistical metrics (RankIC, Information Ratio, turnover), and structured diagnostics are fed back to the LLM for iterative refinement across multiple rounds.
AI.result: In experiments with 30 U.S. equities over 752 trading days, the system evaluated 181 syntactically valid factors from 122 unique candidates across three rounds, achieving a peak composite score of 0.827 with 100% computational stability.
AI.conclusion: Combining LLM-driven generation with deterministic safety constraints yields an effective, interpretable, and reproducible approach to automated factor discovery, addressing the shortcomings of traditional methods like genetic programming.

title: Wolkowicz-Styan Upper Bound on the Hessian Eigenspectrum for Cross-Entropy Loss in Nonlinear Smooth Neural Networks
summary: Neural networks (NNs) are central to modern machine learning and achieve state-of-the-art results in many applications. However, the relationship between loss geometry and generalization is still not well understood. The local geometry of the loss function near a critical point is well-approximated by its quadratic form, obtained through a second-order Taylor expansion. The coefficients of the quadratic term correspond to the Hessian matrix, whose eigenspectrum allows us to evaluate the sharpness of the loss at the critical point. Extensive research suggests flat critical points generalize better, while sharp ones lead to higher generalization error. However, sharpness requires the Hessian eigenspectrum, but general matrix characteristic equations have no closed-form solution. Therefore, most existing studies on evaluating loss sharpness rely on numerical approximation methods. Existing closed-form analyses of the eigenspectrum are primarily limited to simplified architectures, such as linear or ReLU-activated networks; consequently, theoretical analysis of smooth nonlinear multilayer neural networks remains limited. Against this background, this study focuses on nonlinear, smooth multilayer neural networks and derives a closed-form upper bound for the maximum eigenvalue of the Hessian with respect to the cross-entropy loss by leveraging the Wolkowicz-Styan bound. Specifically, the derived upper bound is expressed as a function of the affine transformation parameters, hidden layer dimensions, and the degree of orthogonality among the training samples. The primary contribution of this paper is an analytical characterization of loss sharpness in smooth nonlinear multilayer neural networks via a closed-form expression, avoiding explicit numerical eigenspectrum computation. We hope that this work provides a small yet meaningful step toward unraveling the mysteries of deep learning.

AI.tldr: This paper derives a closed-form upper bound for the maximum eigenvalue of the Hessian (loss sharpness) in smooth nonlinear multilayer neural networks, avoiding numerical approximation methods.
AI.motivation: The relationship between loss geometry and generalization is not well understood, especially for smooth nonlinear multilayer neural networks. While research shows flat critical points generalize better, existing analyses are limited to simplified architectures or require numerical approximations, creating a gap for theoretical analysis of general smooth nonlinear networks.
AI.method: The study leverages the Wolkowicz-Styan bound to derive a closed-form upper bound for the maximum eigenvalue of the Hessian with respect to cross-entropy loss. The bound is expressed as a function of affine transformation parameters, hidden layer dimensions, and the degree of orthogonality among training samples.
AI.result: The paper successfully obtains an analytical characterization of loss sharpness via a closed-form expression that depends on network parameters (affine transformations, hidden dimensions) and data properties (training sample orthogonality), eliminating the need for explicit numerical eigenspectrum computation.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a theoretical foundation for analyzing loss sharpness in smooth nonlinear multilayer neural networks through closed-form expressions, offering a meaningful step toward better understanding deep learning's generalization properties beyond numerical approximations.

title: From Scalars to Tensors: Declared Losses Recover Epistemic Distinctions That Neutrosophic Scalars Cannot Express
summary: Leyva-Vázquez and Smarandache (2025) demonstrated that neutrosophic T/I/F evaluation, where Truth, Indeterminacy, and Falsity are independent dimensions not constrained to sum to 1.0, which reveals "hyper-truth"' (T+I+F > 1.0) in 35% of complex epistemic cases evaluated by LLMs. We extend their work in two directions. First, we replicate and extend their experiment across five model families from five vendors (Anthropic, Meta, DeepSeek, Alibaba, Mistral), finding hyper-truth in 84% of unconstrained evaluations, which confirms the phenomenon is cross-vendor under our prompt protocol. Second, and more significantly, we identify a limitation of scalar T/I/F that their framework cannot address: models adopting an `"Absorption" position (T=0, I=1, F=0) produce identical scalar outputs for fundamentally different epistemic situations (paradox, ignorance, contingency), collapsing the very distinctions neutrosophic logic was designed to preserve. We demonstrate that extending the evaluation to include declared losses (structured descriptions of what the model cannot evaluate and why) substantially recovers these distinctions. Models producing identical scalars for paradox and ignorance produce nearly disjoint loss vocabularies (Jaccard similarity < 0.10 on loss description keywords), with domain-specific, severity-rated loss declarations that differentiate the nature of their uncertainty. This suggests that scalar T/I/F is a necessary but insufficient representation of epistemic state, and that tensor-structured output (scalars + losses) provides a more faithful model of LLM epistemic capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper extends neutrosophic T/I/F evaluation of LLMs across multiple vendors, confirms widespread "hyper-truth" phenomenon, identifies limitations of scalar representation, and proposes tensor-structured output with loss declarations to better capture epistemic distinctions.
AI.motivation: To extend Leyva-Vázquez and Smarandache's (2025) work on neutrosophic T/I/F evaluation of LLMs by validating findings across multiple model families/vendors and addressing the limitation that scalar T/I/F cannot distinguish between fundamentally different epistemic situations (paradox, ignorance, contingency) that produce identical scalar outputs.
AI.method: 1) Replicated and extended the original experiment across five model families from five different vendors (Anthropic, Meta, DeepSeek, Alibaba, Mistral). 2) Introduced "loss declarations" - structured descriptions of what models cannot evaluate and why - to extend evaluation beyond scalar T/I/F outputs. 3) Used Jaccard similarity on loss description keywords to compare models' uncertainty representations. 4) Implemented domain-specific, severity-rated loss declarations.
AI.result: 1) Found hyper-truth (T+I+F > 1.0) in 84% of unconstrained evaluations across all vendors, confirming the phenomenon is cross-vendor. 2) Demonstrated that scalar T/I/F alone collapses distinctions between paradox, ignorance, and contingency (all produce T=0, I=1, F=0). 3) Showed that adding loss declarations recovers these distinctions: models produce nearly disjoint loss vocabularies (Jaccard similarity < 0.10) for paradox vs. ignorance, with domain-specific loss descriptions that differentiate uncertainty nature.
AI.conclusion: Scalar T/I/F is necessary but insufficient for representing LLM epistemic states. Tensor-structured output (combining scalars with structured loss declarations) provides a more faithful model of LLM epistemic capabilities by preserving distinctions between different types of uncertainty that scalar representations collapse.

title: Mild Over-Parameterization Benefits Asymmetric Tensor PCA
summary: Asymmetric Tensor PCA (ATPCA) is a prototypical model for studying the trade-offs between sample complexity, computation, and memory. Existing algorithms for this problem typically require at least $d^{\left\lceil\overline{k}/2\right\rceil}$ state memory cost to recover the signal, where $d$ is the vector dimension and $\overline{k}$ is the tensor order. We focus on the setting where $\overline{k} \geq 4$ is even and consider (stochastic) gradient descent-based algorithms under a limited memory budget, which permits only mild over-parameterization of the model. We propose a matrix-parameterized method (in $d^{2}$ state memory cost) using a novel three-phase alternating-update algorithm to address the problem and demonstrate how mild over-parameterization facilitates learning in two key aspects: (i) it improves sample efficiency, allowing our method to achieve \emph{near-optimal} $d^{\overline{k}-2}$ sample complexity in our limited memory setting; and (ii) it enhances adaptivity to problem structure, a previously unrecognized phenomenon, where the required sample size naturally decreases as consecutive vectors become more aligned, and in the symmetric limit attains $d^{\overline{k}/2}$, matching the \emph{best} known polynomial-time complexity. To our knowledge, this is the \emph{first} tractable algorithm for ATPCA with $d^{\overline{k}}$-independent memory costs.

AI.tldr: A new algorithm for Asymmetric Tensor PCA that achieves near-optimal sample complexity with only d² memory cost, showing how mild over-parameterization improves both sample efficiency and adaptivity to problem structure.
AI.motivation: Existing algorithms for Asymmetric Tensor PCA require at least d^{⌈k̄/2⌉} memory, which is computationally expensive for high-order tensors. There's a need for more memory-efficient methods that work under limited memory budgets while maintaining good performance.
AI.method: A matrix-parameterized method using a novel three-phase alternating-update algorithm based on (stochastic) gradient descent, operating under mild over-parameterization constraints within limited memory budget (d² state memory cost).
AI.result: The method achieves near-optimal d^{k̄-2} sample complexity, shows adaptivity to problem structure (requiring fewer samples as consecutive vectors become more aligned), and in symmetric limit attains d^{k̄/2} sample complexity matching best known polynomial-time complexity. This is the first tractable ATPCA algorithm with d^{k̄}-independent memory costs.
AI.conclusion: Mild over-parameterization enables memory-efficient tensor PCA with improved sample efficiency and structural adaptivity, providing a practical solution for high-order tensor problems where memory is constrained.

title: LLMs for Text-Based Exploration and Navigation Under Partial Observability
comment: 15 pages, (to be published Springer Lecture Notes of the Institute for Computer Sciences, Social Informatics and Telecommunications Engineering [LNICST] )
summary: Exploration and goal-directed navigation in unknown layouts are central to inspection, logistics, and search-and-rescue. We ask whether large language models (LLMs) can function as \emph{text-only} controllers under partial observability -- without code execution, tools, or program synthesis. We introduce a reproducible benchmark with oracle localisation in fixed ASCII gridworlds: each step reveals only a local $5\times5$ window around the agent and the model must select one of \texttt{UP/RIGHT/DOWN/LEFT}. Nine contemporary LLMs ranging from open/proprietary, dense / Mixture of Experts and instruction- vs. reasoning-tuned are evaluated on two tasks across three layouts of increasing difficulty: \emph{Exploration} (maximising revealed cells) and \emph{Navigation} (reach the goal on the shortest path). The experimental results are evaluated on quantitative metrics including \emph{success rate}, \emph{efficiency} such as normalised coverage and \emph{path length} vs. oracle as well as qualitative analysis. Reasoning-tuned models reliably complete navigation across all layouts, yet remain less efficient than oracle paths. Few-shot demonstrations in the prompt chiefly help these Reasoning-tuned models by reducing invalid moves and shortening paths, while classic dense instruction models remain inconsistent. We observe characteristic action priors (UP/RIGHT) that can induce looping under partial observability. Overall, training regimen and test-time deliberation predict control ability better than raw parameter count. These findings suggest lightweight hybridisation with classical online planners as a practical route to deployable partial map systems.

AI.tldr: LLMs can function as text-only controllers for exploration and navigation in partially observable ASCII gridworlds, with reasoning-tuned models performing best but less efficient than optimal paths. Training regimen and test-time deliberation matter more than parameter count.
AI.motivation: Exploration and goal-directed navigation are crucial for inspection, logistics, and search-and-rescue operations. The paper investigates whether LLMs can serve as controllers under partial observability without requiring code execution, tools, or program synthesis, using only text-based interactions.
AI.method: Created a reproducible benchmark with oracle localization in fixed ASCII gridworlds. Each step reveals only a local 5×5 window around the agent, and models select from UP/RIGHT/DOWN/LEFT actions. Evaluated nine contemporary LLMs (open/proprietary, dense/Mixture of Experts, instruction vs. reasoning-tuned) on Exploration (maximize revealed cells) and Navigation (reach goal via shortest path) tasks across three layouts of increasing difficulty.
AI.result: Reasoning-tuned models reliably complete navigation across all layouts but remain less efficient than oracle paths. Few-shot demonstrations help reasoning-tuned models by reducing invalid moves and shortening paths, while classic dense instruction models remain inconsistent. Models show characteristic action priors (UP/RIGHT) that can cause looping under partial observability.
AI.conclusion: Training regimen and test-time deliberation predict control ability better than raw parameter count. Lightweight hybridization with classical online planners is suggested as a practical route to deployable partial map systems.

title: Exploring the impact of fairness-aware criteria in AutoML
summary: Machine Learning (ML) systems are increasingly used to support decision-making processes that affect individuals. However, these systems often rely on biased data, which can lead to unfair outcomes against specific groups. With the growing adoption of Automated Machine Learning (AutoML), the risk of intensifying discriminatory behaviours increases, as most frameworks primarily focus on model selection to maximise predictive performance. Previous research on fairness in AutoML had largely followed this trend, integrating fairness awareness only in the model selection or hyperparameter tuning, while neglecting other critical stages of the ML pipeline. This paper aims to study the impact of integrating fairness directly into the optimisation component of an AutoML framework that constructs complete ML pipelines, from data selection and transformations to model selection and tuning. As selecting appropriate fairness metrics remains a key challenge, our work incorporates complementary fairness metrics to capture different dimensions of fairness during the optimisation. Their integration within AutoML resulted in measurable differences compared to a baseline focused solely on predictive performance. Despite a 9.4% decrease in predictive power, the average fairness improved by 14.5%, accompanied by a 35.7% reduction in data usage. Furthermore, fairness integration produced complete yet simpler final solutions, suggesting that model complexity is not always required to achieve balanced and fair ML solutions.

AI.tldr: This paper integrates multiple fairness metrics into AutoML optimization, resulting in fairer ML pipelines with reduced data usage and simpler models despite some performance trade-off.
AI.motivation: AutoML frameworks traditionally focus on maximizing predictive performance, potentially intensifying discriminatory behaviors from biased data. Existing fairness approaches in AutoML only address model selection or hyperparameter tuning, neglecting other critical pipeline stages.
AI.method: Integrate complementary fairness metrics directly into AutoML optimization component to construct complete ML pipelines (data selection, transformations, model selection, tuning). Uses multiple fairness metrics to capture different fairness dimensions during optimization.
AI.result: Compared to performance-only baseline: 9.4% decrease in predictive power, 14.5% average fairness improvement, 35.7% reduction in data usage. Fairness integration produced complete yet simpler final solutions.
AI.conclusion: Model complexity is not always required to achieve balanced and fair ML solutions. Integrating fairness into AutoML optimization can produce simpler, more efficient, and fairer pipelines while managing performance trade-offs.

title: Evaluating Reliability Gaps in Large Language Model Safety via Repeated Prompt Sampling
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures; accepted at the CCAI 2026 conference
summary: Traditional benchmarks for large language models (LLMs), such as HELM and AIR-BENCH, primarily assess safety risk through breadth-oriented evaluation across diverse tasks. However, real-world deployment often exposes a different class of risk: operational failures arising from repeated generations of the same prompt rather than broad task generalization. In high-stakes settings, response consistency and safety under repeated use are critical operational requirements. We introduce Accelerated Prompt Stress Testing (APST), a depth-oriented evaluation framework inspired by highly accelerated stress testing in reliability engineering. APST probes LLM behavior by repeatedly sampling identical prompts under controlled operational conditions, including temperature variation and prompt perturbation, to surface latent failure modes such as hallucinations, refusal inconsistency, and unsafe completions. Rather than treating failures as isolated events, APST characterizes them statistically as stochastic outcomes of repeated inference. We model observed safety failures using Bernoulli and binomial formulations to estimate per-inference failure probabilities, enabling quantitative comparison of operational risk across models and configurations. We apply APST to multiple instruction-tuned LLMs evaluated on AIR-BENCH 2024 derived safety and security prompts. While models exhibit similar performance under conventional single- or very-low-sample evaluation (N <= 3), repeated sampling reveals substantial variation in empirical failure probabilities across temperatures. These results demonstrate that shallow benchmark scores can obscure meaningful differences in reliability under sustained use.

AI.tldr: APST is a framework for evaluating LLM safety through repeated prompt sampling to measure operational failure probabilities, revealing reliability differences hidden by traditional benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Traditional LLM benchmarks evaluate safety across diverse tasks (breadth), but real-world deployment risks involve operational failures from repeated generations of the same prompt. Response consistency and safety under repeated use are critical in high-stakes settings.
AI.method: Accelerated Prompt Stress Testing (APST) - a depth-oriented framework inspired by reliability engineering. It repeatedly samples identical prompts under controlled conditions (temperature variation, prompt perturbation) to surface latent failure modes like hallucinations, refusal inconsistency, and unsafe completions. Failures are modeled statistically using Bernoulli and binomial formulations to estimate per-inference failure probabilities.
AI.result: APST applied to multiple instruction-tuned LLMs on AIR-BENCH 2024 safety prompts shows that while models perform similarly under conventional low-sample evaluation (N ≤ 3), repeated sampling reveals substantial variation in empirical failure probabilities across temperatures. Shallow benchmark scores obscure meaningful reliability differences under sustained use.
AI.conclusion: APST provides a novel depth-oriented approach to LLM safety evaluation that reveals operational reliability risks hidden by traditional breadth-oriented benchmarks. This framework enables quantitative comparison of operational risk across models and configurations, crucial for high-stakes deployment.

title: A Multi-head Attention Fusion Network for Industrial Prognostics under Discrete Operational Conditions
summary: Complex systems such as aircraft engines, turbines, and industrial machinery often operate under dynamically changing conditions. These varying operating conditions can substantially influence degradation behavior and make prognostic modeling more challenging, as accurate prediction requires explicit consideration of operational effects. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel multi-head attention-based fusion neural network. The proposed framework explicitly models and integrates three signal components: (1) the monotonic degradation trend, which reflects the underlying deterioration of the system; (2) discrete operating states, identified through clustering and encoded into dense embeddings; and (3) residual random noise, which captures unexplained variation in sensor measurements. The core strength of the framework lies in its architecture, which combines BiLSTM networks with attention mechanisms to better capture complex temporal dependencies. The attention mechanism allows the model to adaptively weight different time steps and sensor signals, improving its ability to extract prognostically relevant information. In addition, a fusion module is designed to integrate the outputs from the degradation-trend branch and the operating-state embeddings, enabling the model to capture their interactions more effectively. The proposed method is validated using a dataset from the NASA repository, and the results demonstrate its effectiveness.

AI.tldr: A novel multi-head attention-based fusion neural network for prognostic modeling that integrates monotonic degradation trends, operating states, and residual noise to predict system health under varying conditions.
AI.motivation: Complex systems like aircraft engines and turbines operate under dynamically changing conditions that substantially influence degradation behavior, making prognostic modeling challenging. Accurate prediction requires explicit consideration of these operational effects.
AI.method: A multi-head attention-based fusion neural network that explicitly models three signal components: (1) monotonic degradation trends, (2) discrete operating states identified through clustering and encoded into dense embeddings, and (3) residual random noise. The architecture combines BiLSTM networks with attention mechanisms and includes a fusion module to integrate degradation trends and operating-state embeddings.
AI.result: The proposed method was validated using a dataset from the NASA repository, and the results demonstrate its effectiveness in prognostic modeling under varying operating conditions.
AI.conclusion: The attention-based fusion framework successfully integrates multiple signal components to improve prognostic accuracy for complex systems operating under dynamically changing conditions, with the attention mechanism enabling adaptive weighting of relevant temporal and sensor information.

title: Unifying Ontology Construction and Semantic Alignment for Deterministic Enterprise Reasoning at Scale
summary: While enterprises amass vast quantities of data, much of it remains chaotic and effectively dormant, preventing decision-making based on comprehensive information. Existing neuro-symbolic approaches rely on disjoint pipelines and struggle with error propagation. We introduce the large ontology model (LOM), a unified framework that seamlessly integrates ontology construction, semantic alignment, and logical reasoning into a single end-to-end architecture. LOM employs a construct-align-reason (CAR) pipeline, leveraging its unified architecture across all three stages: it first autonomously constructs a domain-specific ontological universe from raw data, then aligns neural generation with this structural reality using a graph-aware encoder and reinforcement learning, and finally executes deterministic reasoning over the constructed topology, node attributes and relation types. We evaluate LOM on a comprehensive benchmark constructed from diverse real-world enterprise datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that LOM-4B achieves 88.8% accuracy in ontology completion and 94% in complex graph reasoning tasks, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art LLMs. These findings validate that autonomous logical construction is essential for achieving deterministic, enterprise-grade intelligence.

AI.tldr: LOM (Large Ontology Model) is a unified neuro-symbolic framework that integrates ontology construction, semantic alignment, and logical reasoning in an end-to-end architecture, achieving superior performance on enterprise data tasks.
AI.motivation: Enterprises have vast chaotic data that remains dormant and unusable for decision-making. Existing neuro-symbolic approaches use disjoint pipelines and suffer from error propagation problems.
