Our results highlight CodeQuant as a promising direction for efficient and accurate deployment of MoE-based large language models under low-precision constraints. Our code is available at https://github.com/SAI-Lab-NYU/CodeQuant.

AI.tldr: CodeQuant is a unified quantization-and-clustering scheme for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models that addresses outlier-induced accuracy degradation in low-precision deployment by combining learnable rotation smoothing with weight outlier absorption into fine-tuned cluster centroids.
AI.motivation: Outliers in MoE architectures cause substantial quantization errors during post-training quantization, leading to severe accuracy degradation that impedes reliable low-precision deployment of large models, despite existing rotation-based smoothing techniques leaving residual errors.
AI.method: CodeQuant combines two main techniques: 1) smoothing activation outliers via learnable rotation, and 2) absorbing weight outliers into fine-tuned cluster centroids. This unified quantization-and-clustering approach reduces the influence of extreme values by fitting them within cluster centroids, lowering quantization error while maintaining expressive capacity.
AI.result: CodeQuant achieves up to 4.15× speedup while delivering significantly higher accuracy than state-of-the-art quantization approaches across diverse MoE models, enabled by a dedicated kernel design for GPU and CPU.
AI.conclusion: CodeQuant represents a promising direction for efficient and accurate deployment of MoE-based large language models under low-precision constraints, effectively addressing the fundamental bottleneck of outliers in quantization.

title: MEMENTO: Teaching LLMs to Manage Their Own Context
summary: Reasoning models think in long, unstructured streams with no mechanism for compressing or organizing their own intermediate state. We introduce MEMENTO: a method that teaches models to segment reasoning into blocks, compress each block into a memento, i.e., a dense state summary, and reason forward by attending only to mementos, reducing context, KV cache, and compute. To train MEMENTO models, we release OpenMementos, a public dataset of 228K reasoning traces derived from OpenThoughts-v3, segmented and annotated with intermediate summaries. We show that a two-stage SFT recipe on OpenMementos is effective across different model families (Qwen3, Phi-4, Olmo 3) and scales (8B--32B parameters). Trained models maintain strong accuracy on math, science, and coding benchmarks while achieving ${\sim}2.5\times$ peak KV cache reduction. We extend vLLM to support our inference method, achieving ${\sim}1.75\times$ throughput improvement while also enabling us to perform RL and further improve accuracy. Finally, we identify a dual information stream: information from each reasoning block is carried both by the memento text and by the corresponding KV states, which retain implicit information from the original block. Removing this channel drops accuracy by 15\,pp on AIME24.

AI.tldr: MEMENTO is a method that teaches language models to segment their reasoning into blocks, compress each block into dense state summaries called mementos, and reason forward by attending only to these mementos, reducing context, KV cache, and computational requirements.
AI.motivation: Current reasoning models think in long, unstructured streams without mechanisms for compressing or organizing their intermediate states, leading to inefficiencies in context usage, KV cache, and compute.
AI.method: Introduces MEMENTO with a two-stage SFT recipe trained on OpenMementos dataset (228K reasoning traces from OpenThoughts-v3). Models learn to segment reasoning into blocks, compress each block into a memento (dense state summary), and reason forward attending only to mementos. Extended vLLM to support inference method.
AI.result: Trained models maintain strong accuracy on math, science, and coding benchmarks while achieving ~2.5× peak KV cache reduction and ~1.75× throughput improvement. Also enables RL for further accuracy improvement. Discovered dual information stream phenomenon where information is carried both by memento text and corresponding KV states.
AI.conclusion: MEMENTO effectively teaches models to organize and compress reasoning states, significantly improving efficiency while maintaining accuracy. The dual information stream discovery shows that KV states retain implicit information from original reasoning blocks, contributing to model performance.

title: PepBenchmark: A Standardized Benchmark for Peptide Machine Learning
summary: Peptide therapeutics are widely regarded as the "third generation" of drugs, yet progress in peptide Machine Learning (ML) are hindered by the absence of standardized benchmarks. Here we present PepBenchmark, which unifies datasets, preprocessing, and evaluation protocols for peptide drug discovery. PepBenchmark comprises three components: (1) PepBenchData, a well-curated collection comprising 29 canonical-peptide and 6 non-canonical-peptide datasets across 7 groups, systematically covering key aspects of peptide drug development, representing, to the best of our knowledge, the most comprehensive AI-ready dataset resource to date; (2) PepBenchPipeline, a standardized preprocessing pipeline that ensures consistent dataset cleaning, construction, splitting, and feature transformation, mitigating quality issues common in ad hoc pipelines; and (3) PepBenchLeaderboard, a unified evaluation protocol and leaderboard with strong baselines across 4 major methodological families: Fingerprint-based, GNN-based, PLM-based, and SMILES-based models. Together, PepBenchmark provides the first standardized and comparable foundation for peptide drug discovery, facilitating methodological advances and translation into real-world applications. The data and code are publicly available at https://github.com/ZGCI-AI4S-Pep/PepBenchmark/.

AI.tldr: PepBenchmark is a comprehensive benchmark suite that standardizes datasets, preprocessing, and evaluation for peptide drug discovery ML, addressing the lack of comparable benchmarks in the field.
AI.motivation: Progress in peptide machine learning is hindered by the absence of standardized benchmarks, making it difficult to compare methods and advance the field systematically.
AI.method: Created PepBenchmark with three components: PepBenchData (35 curated peptide datasets across 7 groups), PepBenchPipeline (standardized preprocessing), and PepBenchLeaderboard (unified evaluation with baselines across 4 methodological families).
AI.result: PepBenchmark provides the first standardized and comparable foundation for peptide drug discovery, representing the most comprehensive AI-ready dataset resource to date with strong baselines across multiple ML approaches.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark facilitates methodological advances and translation into real-world applications by providing standardized datasets, preprocessing, and evaluation protocols for the peptide ML community.

title: Instructing LLMs to Negotiate using Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
summary: The recent advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has established their potential as autonomous interactive agents. However, they often struggle in strategic games of incomplete information, such as bilateral price negotiation. In this paper, we investigate if Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) can effectively teach LLMs to negotiate. Specifically, we explore the strategic behaviors that emerge during the learning process. We introduce a framework that trains a mid-sized buyer agent against a regulated LLM seller across a wide distribution of real-world products. By grounding reward signals directly in the maximization of economic surplus and strict adherence to private budget constraints, we reveal a novel four-phase strategic evolution. The agent progresses from naive bargaining to using aggressive starting prices, moves through a phase of deadlock, and ultimately develops sophisticated persuasive skills. Our results demonstrate that this verifiable training allows a 30B agent to significantly outperform frontier models over ten times its size in extracting surplus. Furthermore, the trained agent generalizes robustly to stronger counterparties unseen during training and remains effective even when facing hostile, adversarial seller personas.

AI.tldr: LLMs trained with Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) can master complex price negotiation strategies, evolving from naive to sophisticated tactics and outperforming much larger models.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models struggle with strategic games of incomplete information like bilateral price negotiation, where private information and strategic behavior matter. Current LLMs lack effective negotiation capabilities despite their general conversational abilities.
AI.method: Introduces RLVR framework that trains a mid-sized buyer agent against a regulated LLM seller across real-world products. Uses verifiable reward signals grounded in maximizing economic surplus while strictly adhering to private budget constraints. The training involves learning negotiation strategies through reinforcement learning with concrete, verifiable outcomes.
AI.result: The agent exhibits a four-phase strategic evolution: 1) naive bargaining, 2) aggressive starting prices, 3) deadlock phase, and 4) sophisticated persuasive skills. A 30B parameter agent significantly outperforms frontier models 10x larger in surplus extraction. The trained agent generalizes robustly to stronger unseen counterparties and remains effective against hostile, adversarial seller personas.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards effectively teaches LLMs complex negotiation strategies, enabling smaller models to outperform much larger ones in strategic incomplete information games. The approach produces robust, generalizable agents capable of sophisticated economic reasoning and persuasion in bilateral negotiations.

title: IceCache: Memory-efficient KV-cache Management for Long-Sequence LLMs
summary: Key-Value (KV) cache plays a crucial role in accelerating inference in large language models (LLMs) by storing intermediate attention states and avoiding redundant computation during autoregressive generation. However, its memory footprint scales linearly with sequence length, often leading to severe memory bottlenecks on resource-constrained hardware. Prior work has explored offloading KV cache to the CPU while retaining only a subset on the GPU, but these approaches often rely on imprecise token selection and suffer performance degradation in long-generation tasks such as chain-of-thought reasoning. In this paper, we propose a novel KV cache management strategy, IceCache, which integrates semantic token clustering with PagedAttention. By organizing semantically related tokens into contiguous memory regions managed by a hierarchical, dynamically updatable data structure, our method enables more efficient token selection and better utilization of memory bandwidth during CPU-GPU transfers. Experimental results on LongBench show that, with a 256-token budget, IceCache maintains 99% of the original accuracy achieved by the full KV cache model. Moreover, compared to other offloading-based methods, IceCache attains competitive or even superior latency and accuracy while using only 25% of the KV cache token budget, demonstrating its effectiveness in long-sequence scenarios. The code is available on our project website at https://yuzhenmao.github.io/IceCache/.

AI.tldr: IceCache: A novel KV cache management strategy using semantic token clustering with PagedAttention to efficiently offload attention states to CPU, reducing memory footprint while maintaining accuracy in long-sequence LLM inference.
AI.motivation: KV cache memory footprint scales linearly with sequence length, causing severe memory bottlenecks on resource-constrained hardware. Prior CPU offloading approaches rely on imprecise token selection and suffer performance degradation in long-generation tasks.
AI.method: IceCache integrates semantic token clustering with PagedAttention, organizing semantically related tokens into contiguous memory regions managed by a hierarchical, dynamically updatable data structure for efficient token selection and better memory bandwidth utilization during CPU-GPU transfers.
AI.result: With 256-token budget, maintains 99% original accuracy of full KV cache model. Using only 25% KV cache token budget, achieves competitive or superior latency and accuracy compared to other offloading methods in LongBench experiments.
AI.conclusion: IceCache effectively addresses memory bottlenecks in LLM inference through intelligent semantic token organization and efficient CPU-GPU transfer, demonstrating practical effectiveness for long-sequence scenarios while maintaining high accuracy.

title: Evolutionary Token-Level Prompt Optimization for Diffusion Models
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models exhibit strong generative performance but remain highly sensitive to prompt formulation, often requiring extensive manual trial and error to obtain satisfactory results. This motivates the development of automated, model-agnostic prompt optimization methods that can systematically explore the conditioning space beyond conventional text rewriting. This work investigates the use of a Genetic Algorithm (GA) for prompt optimization by directly evolving the token vectors employed by CLIP-based diffusion models. The GA optimizes a fitness function that combines aesthetic quality, measured by the LAION Aesthetic Predictor V2, with prompt-image alignment, assessed via CLIPScore. Experiments on 36 prompts from the Parti Prompts (P2) dataset show that the proposed approach outperforms the baseline methods, including Promptist and random search, achieving up to a 23.93% improvement in fitness. Overall, the method is adaptable to image generation models with tokenized text encoders and provides a modular framework for future extensions, the limitations and prospects of which are discussed.

AI.tldr: A genetic algorithm approach that directly evolves token vectors in CLIP-based diffusion models to optimize prompts for better aesthetic quality and alignment, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models are highly sensitive to prompt formulation, requiring extensive manual trial and error to get good results, creating a need for automated, model-agnostic prompt optimization methods.
AI.method: Uses a Genetic Algorithm to directly evolve token vectors employed by CLIP-based diffusion models, optimizing a fitness function combining aesthetic quality (LAION Aesthetic Predictor V2) with prompt-image alignment (CLIPScore).
AI.result: Experiments on 36 prompts from Parti Prompts (P2) dataset show the approach outperforms baseline methods including Promptist and random search, achieving up to 23.93% improvement in fitness.
AI.conclusion: The method is adaptable to image generation models with tokenized text encoders, provides a modular framework for future extensions, and discussions of limitations and prospects are included.

title: WaveMoE: A Wavelet-Enhanced Mixture-of-Experts Foundation Model for Time Series Forecasting
comment: Presented at ICLR 2026 TSALM Workshop (1st Workshop on Time Series in the Age of Large Models)
summary: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) have recently achieved remarkable success in universal forecasting by leveraging large-scale pretraining on diverse time series data. Complementing this progress, incorporating frequency-domain information yields promising performance in enhancing the modeling of complex temporal patterns, such as periodicity and localized high-frequency dynamics, which are prevalent in real-world time series. To advance this direction, we propose a new perspective that integrates explicit frequency-domain representations into scalable foundation models, and introduce WaveMoE, a wavelet-enhanced mixture-of-experts foundation model for time series forecasting. WaveMoE adopts a dual-path architecture that jointly processes time series tokens and wavelet tokens aligned along a unified temporal axis, and coordinates them through a shared expert routing mechanism that enables consistent expert specialization while efficiently scaling model capacity. Preliminary experimental results on 16 diverse benchmark datasets indicate that WaveMoE has the potential to further improve forecasting performance by incorporating wavelet-domain corpora.

AI.tldr: WaveMoE is a wavelet-enhanced mixture-of-experts foundation model for time series forecasting that integrates frequency-domain representations with scalable foundation models using a dual-path architecture.
AI.motivation: Time series foundation models have achieved success but can benefit from incorporating frequency-domain information to better capture complex temporal patterns like periodicity and high-frequency dynamics common in real-world time series.
AI.method: Proposes WaveMoE with a dual-path architecture that jointly processes time series tokens and wavelet tokens aligned along a unified temporal axis, coordinated through a shared expert routing mechanism that enables consistent expert specialization while scaling model capacity efficiently.
AI.result: Preliminary experimental results on 16 diverse benchmark datasets indicate that WaveMoE has the potential to further improve forecasting performance by incorporating wavelet-domain corpora.
AI.conclusion: Integrating explicit frequency-domain representations into scalable foundation models via the proposed WaveMoE architecture represents a promising direction for advancing time series forecasting performance.

title: What do your logits know? (The answer may surprise you!)
summary: Recent work has shown that probing model internals can reveal a wealth of information not apparent from the model generations. This poses the risk of unintentional or malicious information leakage, where model users are able to learn information that the model owner assumed was inaccessible. Using vision-language models as a testbed, we present the first systematic comparison of information retained at different "representational levels'' as it is compressed from the rich information encoded in the residual stream through two natural bottlenecks: low-dimensional projections of the residual stream obtained using tuned lens, and the final top-k logits most likely to impact model's answer. We show that even easily accessible bottlenecks defined by the model's top logit values can leak task-irrelevant information present in an image-based query, in some cases revealing as much information as direct projections of the full residual stream.

AI.tldr: Vision-language models can leak sensitive information through various output representations, with even simple bottlenecks like top-k logits revealing as much private information as full residual stream projections.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the security risk of information leakage in AI models, where probing model internals can reveal unintended information that model owners assumed was inaccessible. This poses risks of unintentional or malicious data extraction from models, particularly in vision-language systems.
AI.method: Using vision-language models as a testbed, the authors systematically compare information retention at different "representational levels" as information is compressed through natural bottlenecks. They analyze two main bottlenecks: 1) low-dimensional projections of the residual stream obtained using tuned lens, and 2) the final top-k logits most likely to impact the model's answer.
AI.result: The study shows that even easily accessible bottlenecks defined by the model's top logit values can leak task-irrelevant information present in image-based queries. In some cases, these simple bottlenecks reveal as much information as direct projections of the full residual stream.
AI.conclusion: Vision-language models have significant information leakage vulnerabilities even through simple output interfaces. Model owners cannot safely assume that limiting access to certain model components prevents information extraction, as even top logits can leak substantial private information.

title: Topology-Aware PAC-Bayesian Generalization Analysis for Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph neural networks have demonstrated excellent applicability to a wide range of domains, including social networks, biological systems, recommendation systems, and wireless communications. Yet a principled theoretical understanding of their generalization behavior remains limited, particularly for graph classification tasks where complex interactions between model parameters and graph structure play a crucial role. Among existing theoretical tools, PAC-Bayesian norm-based generalization bounds provide a flexible and data-dependent framework; however, current results for GNNs often restrict the exploitation of graph structures. In this work, we propose a topology-aware PAC-Bayesian norm-based generalization framework for graph convolutional networks (GCNs) that extends a previously developed framework to graph-structured models. Our approach reformulates the derivation of generalization bounds as a stochastic optimization problem and introduces sensitivity matrices that measure the response of classification outputs with respect to structured weight perturbations. By imposing different structures on sensitivity matrices from both spatial and spectral perspectives, we derive a family of generalization error bounds with graph structures explicitly embedded. Such bounds could recover existing results as special cases, while yielding bounds that are tighter than state-of-the-art PAC-Bayesian bounds for GNNs. Notably, the proposed framework explicitly integrates graph structural properties into the generalization analysis, enabling a unified inspection of GNN generalization behavior from both spatial aggregation and spectral filtering viewpoints.

AI.tldr: A novel topology-aware PAC-Bayesian framework for analyzing generalization bounds in graph convolutional networks that explicitly incorporates graph structural properties from both spatial and spectral perspectives.
AI.motivation: Graph neural networks show excellent performance across domains, but theoretical understanding of their generalization behavior remains limited. Existing PAC-Bayesian bounds for GNNs often fail to fully exploit graph structural information, particularly for graph classification tasks where complex interactions between model parameters and graph structure are crucial.
AI.method: Proposes a topology-aware PAC-Bayesian norm-based generalization framework that: 1) Reformulates derivation of generalization bounds as stochastic optimization problem, 2) Introduces sensitivity matrices measuring classification output responses to structured weight perturbations, 3) Imposes different structures on sensitivity matrices from spatial and spectral perspectives, 4) Derives a family of generalization error bounds with explicit graph structure embedding.
AI.result: The framework yields generalization bounds that can recover existing results as special cases while producing bounds tighter than state-of-the-art PAC-Bayesian bounds for GNNs. It explicitly integrates graph structural properties into generalization analysis, enabling unified inspection from both spatial aggregation and spectral filtering viewpoints.
AI.conclusion: The proposed topology-aware PAC-Bayesian framework provides a principled theoretical approach to understanding GNN generalization by explicitly incorporating graph structure, offering tighter bounds and unified analysis capabilities that advance theoretical understanding of graph neural network behavior.

title: In-situ process monitoring for defect detection in wire-arc additive manufacturing: an agentic AI approach
summary: AI agents are being increasingly deployed across a wide range of real-world applications. In this paper, we propose an agentic AI framework for in-situ process monitoring for defect detection in wire-arc additive manufacturing (WAAM). The autonomous agent leverages a WAAM process monitoring dataset and a trained classification tool to build AI agents and uses a large language model (LLM) for in-situ process monitoring decision-making for defect detection. A processing agent is developed based on welder process signals, such as current and voltage, and a monitoring agent is developed based on acoustic data collected during the process. Both agents are tasked with identifying porosity defects from processing and monitoring signals, respectively. Ground truth X-ray computed tomography (XCT) data are used to develop classification tools for both the processing and monitoring agents. Furthermore, a multi-agent framework is demonstrated in which the processing and monitoring agents are orchestrated together for parallel decision-making on the given task of defect classification. Evaluation metrics are proposed to determine the efficacy of both individual agents, the combined single-agent, and the coordinated multi-agent system. The multi-agent configuration outperforms all individual-agent counterparts, achieving a decision accuracy of 91.6% and an F1 score of 0.821 on decided runs, across 15 independent runs, and a reasoning quality score of 3.74 out of 5. These in-situ process monitoring agents hold significant potential for autonomous real-time process monitoring and control toward building qualified parts for WAAM and other additive manufacturing processes.

AI.tldr: AI agents for in-situ defect detection in wire-arc additive manufacturing using process signals and acoustic data, with multi-agent framework outperforming individual agents.
AI.motivation: AI agents are increasingly deployed in real-world applications, and there's a need for autonomous in-situ process monitoring for defect detection in wire-arc additive manufacturing (WAAM) to build qualified parts.
AI.method: Developed two AI agents: processing agent based on welder process signals (current, voltage) and monitoring agent based on acoustic data. Used ground truth X-ray CT data to train classification tools. Created multi-agent framework where both agents are orchestrated for parallel decision-making on defect classification.
AI.result: Multi-agent configuration achieved 91.6% decision accuracy, F1 score of 0.821 on decided runs across 15 independent runs, and reasoning quality score of 3.74/5, outperforming all individual-agent counterparts.
AI.conclusion: The in-situ process monitoring agents show significant potential for autonomous real-time process monitoring and control in WAAM and other additive manufacturing processes, with multi-agent coordination providing superior performance.

title: Heterogeneous Connectivity in Sparse Networks: Fan-in Profiles, Gradient Hierarchy, and Topological Equilibria
summary: Profiled Sparse Networks (PSN) replace uniform connectivity with deterministic, heterogeneous fan-in profiles defined by continuous, nonlinear functions, creating neurons with both dense and sparse receptive fields. We benchmark PSN across four classification datasets spanning vision and tabular domains, input dimensions from 54 to 784, and network depths of 2--3 hidden layers. At 90% sparsity, all static profiles, including the uniform random baseline, achieve accuracy within 0.2-0.6% of dense baselines on every dataset, demonstrating that heterogeneous connectivity provides no accuracy advantage when hub placement is arbitrary rather than task-aligned. This result holds across sparsity levels (80-99.9%), profile shapes (eight parametric families, lognormal, and power-law), and fan-in coefficients of variation from 0 to 2.5. Internal gradient analysis reveals that structured profiles create a 2-5x gradient concentration at hub neurons compared to the ~1x uniform distribution in random baselines, with the hierarchy strength predicted by fan-in coefficient of variation ($r = 0.93$). When PSN fan-in distributions are used to initialise RigL dynamic sparse training, lognormal profiles matched to the equilibrium fan-in distribution consistently outperform standard ERK initialisation, with advantages growing on harder tasks, achieving +0.16% on Fashion-MNIST ($p = 0.036$, $d = 1.07$), +0.43% on EMNIST, and +0.49% on Forest Cover. RigL converges to a characteristic fan-in distribution regardless of initialisation. Starting at this equilibrium allows the optimiser to refine weights rather than rearrange topology. Which neurons become hubs matters more than the degree of connectivity variance, i.e., random hub placement provides no advantage, while optimisation-driven placement does.

AI.tldr: PSNs use deterministic heterogeneous connectivity profiles instead of uniform random connections, but arbitrary hub placement provides no accuracy advantage over random sparse networks. However, using task-aligned fan-in distributions for initialization improves dynamic sparse training performance.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates whether structured sparse connectivity with heterogeneous fan-in profiles (having both dense and sparse neurons) provides accuracy advantages over random sparse connectivity. It aims to understand if deterministic, non-uniform connectivity patterns are beneficial for neural network performance.
AI.method: The authors benchmark PSNs across four classification datasets with varying input dimensions and network depths. They test different sparsity levels (80-99.9%), profile shapes (eight parametric families including lognormal and power-law), and fan-in coefficient of variation. They also use PSN fan-in distributions to initialize RigL dynamic sparse training and analyze internal gradient distributions.
AI.result: At 90% sparsity, all static profiles (including uniform random baseline) achieve accuracy within 0.2-0.6% of dense baselines, showing no advantage for arbitrary hub placement. However, using lognormal profiles matched to RigL's equilibrium fan-in distribution improves dynamic sparse training performance (+0.16-0.49% across datasets). Structured profiles create 2-5x gradient concentration at hub neurons compared to uniform distribution in random baselines.
AI.conclusion: Which neurons become hubs matters more than the degree of connectivity variance - random hub placement provides no advantage, while optimization-driven placement does. Starting at the equilibrium fan-in distribution allows optimizers to refine weights rather than rearrange topology, improving performance in dynamic sparse training.

title: GLEaN: A Text-to-image Bias Detection Approach for Public Comprehension
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) models, and their encoded biases, increasingly shape the visual media the public encounters. While researchers have produced a rich body of work on bias measurement, auditing, and mitigation in T2I systems, those methods largely target technical stakeholders, leaving a gap in public legibility. We introduce GLEaN (Generative Likeness Evaluation at N-Scale), a portrait-based explainability pipeline designed to make T2I model biases visually understandable to a broad audience. GLEaN comprises three stages: automated large-scale image generation from identity prompts, facial landmark-based filtering and spatial alignment, and median-pixel composition that distills a model's central tendency into a single representative portrait. The resulting composites require no statistical background to interpret; a viewer can see, at a glance, who a model 'imagines' when prompted with 'a doctor' versus a 'felon.' We demonstrate GLEaN on Stable Diffusion XL across 40 social and occupational identity prompts, producing composites that reproduce documented biases and surface new associations between skin tone and predicted emotion. We find in a between-subjects user study (N = 291) that GLEaN portraits communicate biases as effectively as conventional data tables, but require significantly less viewing time. Because the method relies solely on generated outputs, it can also be replicated on any black-box and closed-weight systems without access to model internals. GLEaN offers a scalable, model-agnostic approach to bias explainability, purpose-built for public comprehension, and is publicly available at https://github.com/cultureiolab/GLEaN.

AI.tldr: GLEaN is a visual explainability pipeline that transforms text-to-image model biases into easily interpretable portrait composites, making complex technical biases accessible to public audiences through intuitive visual representations.
AI.motivation: Current bias measurement, auditing, and mitigation methods for text-to-image models are largely targeted at technical stakeholders, creating a significant gap in public legibility and understanding of how these increasingly influential models encode and reproduce societal biases.
AI.method: GLEaN employs a three-stage portrait-based pipeline: 1) automated large-scale image generation from identity prompts, 2) facial landmark-based filtering and spatial alignment of generated portraits, and 3) median-pixel composition that distills a model's central tendencies into single representative portraits.
AI.result: The method effectively visualizes Stable Diffusion XL biases across 40 social/occupational identities, reproduces documented biases, reveals new skin tone-emotion associations, and user studies show GLEaN communicates biases as effectively as data tables but with significantly faster comprehension.
AI.conclusion: GLEaN provides a scalable, model-agnostic approach to bias explainability that works on black-box systems, bridges the public comprehension gap, and represents a practical tool for making AI biases visually accessible to broad audiences.

title: ReadMOF: Structure-Free Semantic Embeddings from Systematic MOF Nomenclature for Machine Learning
summary: Systematic chemical names, such as IUPAC-style nomenclature for metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), contain rich structural and compositional information in a standardized textual format. Here we introduce ReadMOF, which is, to our knowledge, the first nomenclature-free machine learning framework that leverages these names to model structure-property relationships without requiring atomic coordinates or connectivity graphs. By employing pretrained language models, ReadMOF converts systematic MOF names from the Cambridge Structural Database (CSD) into vector embeddings that closely represent traditional structure-based descriptors. These embeddings enable applications in materials informatics, including property prediction, similarity retrieval, and clustering, with performance comparable to geometry-dependent methods. When combined with large language models, ReadMOF also establishes chemically meaningful reasoning ability with textual input only. Our results show that structured chemical language, interpreted through modern natural language processing techniques, can provide a scalable, interpretable, and geometry-independent alternative to conventional molecular representations. This approach opens new opportunities for language-driven discovery in materials science.

AI.tldr: ReadMOF is the first machine learning framework that uses IUPAC-style systematic names of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) instead of atomic coordinates or graphs to model structure-property relationships, achieving performance comparable to geometry-dependent methods.
AI.motivation: Systematic chemical names for MOFs contain rich structural and compositional information in a standardized textual format, but this information has been underutilized for machine learning. Current methods require atomic coordinates or connectivity graphs, which can be computationally expensive and complex to process.
AI.method: ReadMOF employs pretrained language models to convert systematic MOF names from the Cambridge Structural Database (CSD) into vector embeddings that represent structure-based descriptors. These embeddings are then used for property prediction, similarity retrieval, and clustering. The framework also combines with large language models to establish chemically meaningful reasoning with textual input only.
AI.result: The embeddings enable materials informatics applications with performance comparable to geometry-dependent methods. The framework shows that systematic chemical names interpreted through NLP can provide scalable, interpretable, and geometry-independent representations of MOFs.
AI.conclusion: Structured chemical language processed with modern NLP techniques offers a viable alternative to conventional molecular representations, opening new opportunities for language-driven discovery in materials science without requiring atomic coordinates.

title: HealthAdminBench: Evaluating Computer-Use Agents on Healthcare Administration Tasks
comment: 24 pages, 5 figures, 5 tables. Benchmark paper introducing 4 simulated environments, 135 tasks, and 1,698 evaluation points for healthcare administrative computer-use agents
summary: Healthcare administration accounts for over $1 trillion in annual spending, making it a promising target for LLM-based computer-use agents (CUAs). While clinical applications of LLMs have received significant attention, no benchmark exists for evaluating CUAs on end-to-end administrative workflows. To address this gap, we introduce HealthAdminBench, a benchmark comprising four realistic GUI environments: an EHR, two payer portals, and a fax system, and 135 expert-defined tasks spanning three administrative task types: Prior Authorization, Appeals and Denials Management, and Durable Medical Equipment (DME) Order Processing. Each task is decomposed into fine-grained, verifiable subtasks, yielding 1,698 evaluation points. We evaluate seven agent configurations under multiple prompting and observation settings and find that, despite strong subtask performance, end-to-end reliability remains low: the best-performing agent (Claude Opus 4.6 CUA) achieves only 36.3 percent task success, while GPT-5.4 CUA attains the highest subtask success rate (82.8 percent). These results reveal a substantial gap between current agent capabilities and the demands of real-world administrative workflows. HealthAdminBench provides a rigorous foundation for evaluating progress toward safe and reliable automation of healthcare administrative workflows.

AI.tldr: HealthAdminBench is a new benchmark for evaluating LLM-based computer-use agents on healthcare administrative workflows, revealing significant gaps between current agent capabilities and real-world requirements despite high subtask performance.
AI.motivation: Healthcare administration costs over $1 trillion annually, making it a promising target for LLM automation, but there's no existing benchmark for evaluating computer-use agents on end-to-end administrative workflows.
AI.method: Created HealthAdminBench with four realistic GUI environments (EHR, two payer portals, fax system) and 135 expert-defined tasks across three administrative areas. Decomposed tasks into 1,698 verifiable subtasks, then evaluated seven agent configurations under various prompting and observation settings.
AI.result: Best-performing agent (Claude Opus 4.6 CUA) achieved only 36.3% end-to-end task success, while GPT-5.4 CUA attained the highest subtask success rate (82.8%). Results show substantial gap between current agent capabilities and real-world workflow demands.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM-based computer-use agents are not yet reliable enough for real-world healthcare administrative workflows despite strong individual subtask performance. HealthAdminBench provides a rigorous foundation for measuring progress toward safe automation.

title: WOODELF-HD: Efficient Background SHAP for High-Depth Decision Trees
summary: Decision-tree ensembles are a cornerstone of predictive modeling, and SHAP is a standard framework for interpreting their predictions. Among its variants, Background SHAP offers high accuracy by modeling missing features using a background dataset. Historically, this approach did not scale well, as the time complexity for explaining n instances using m background samples included an O(mn) component. Recent methods such as Woodelf and PLTreeSHAP reduce this to O(m+n), but introduce a preprocessing bottleneck that grows as 3^D with tree depth D, making them impractical for deep trees. We address this limitation with WoodelfHD, a Woodelf extension that reduces the 3^D factor to 2^D. The key idea is a Strassen-like multiplication scheme that exploits the structure of Woodelf matrices, reducing matrix-vector multiplication from O(k^2) to O(k*log(k)) via a fully vectorized, non-recursive implementation. In addition, we merge path nodes with identical features, reducing cache size and memory usage. When running on standard environments, WoodelfHD enables exact Background SHAP computation for trees with depths up to 21, where previous methods fail due to excessive memory usage. For ensembles of depths 12 and 15, it achieves speedups of 33x and 162x, respectively, over the state-of-the-art.

AI.tldr: WoodelfHD extends Woodelf to enable exact Background SHAP computation for deep decision trees by reducing the exponential growth factor from 3^D to 2^D using Strassen-like multiplication and path node optimization.
AI.motivation: While Background SHAP provides high accuracy for interpreting decision-tree ensemble predictions, it suffers from scalability issues. Traditional methods had O(mn) complexity, and recent approaches like Woodelf and PLTreeSHAP reduced this to O(m+n) but introduced a preprocessing bottleneck that grows as 3^D with tree depth D, making them impractical for deep trees.
AI.method: WoodelfHD extends Woodelf with two key innovations: 1) A Strassen-like multiplication scheme that exploits the structure of Woodelf matrices, reducing matrix-vector multiplication from O(k^2) to O(k*log(k)) via fully vectorized, non-recursive implementation; 2) Merging path nodes with identical features to reduce cache size and memory usage.
AI.result: WoodelfHD enables exact Background SHAP computation for trees with depths up to 21 (previous methods failed due to excessive memory). For ensembles of depths 12 and 15, it achieves speedups of 33x and 162x respectively over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: WoodelfHD successfully addresses the scalability limitation of Background SHAP for deep decision trees by significantly reducing both computational complexity and memory requirements, making exact SHAP explanations practical for much deeper tree ensembles than previously possible.

title: New Hybrid Fine-Tuning Paradigm for LLMs: Algorithm Design and Convergence Analysis Framework
summary: Fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs) typically involves either full fine-tuning, which updates all model parameters, or Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT), which adjusts a small subset of parameters. However, both approaches have inherent limitations: full fine-tuning is computationally expensive, while PEFT often struggles to learn new knowledge and exhibits suboptimal performance. To overcome these issues, we propose a novel hybrid fine-tuning approach that jointly updates both LLMs and PEFT modules using a combination of zeroth-order and first-order optimization methods. To analyze our new algorithm, we develop a theoretical framework centered on the concept of hybrid smoothness condition, which accounts for the heterogeneous nature of the optimization landscape in joint LLM and PEFT training. We derive a rigorous convergence analysis for the convergence of reshuffling-type SGD algorithm under multiple learning rates and demonstrate its effectiveness through extensive empirical studies across various downstream tasks and model architectures. On the practical side, our results demonstrate consistent performance improvement, making the approach a viable solution for large-scale language model fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: Hybrid fine-tuning approach combining zeroth-order and first-order optimization for joint updating of LLMs and PEFT modules, with theoretical analysis using hybrid smoothness condition.
AI.motivation: Full fine-tuning is computationally expensive, while PEFT struggles to learn new knowledge and has suboptimal performance. Need a solution that overcomes limitations of both approaches.
AI.method: Novel hybrid fine-tuning approach that jointly updates LLMs and PEFT modules using combination of zeroth-order and first-order optimization methods. Theoretical framework based on hybrid smoothness condition for analyzing heterogeneous optimization landscape.
AI.result: Developed theoretical framework and convergence analysis for reshuffling-type SGD algorithm under multiple learning rates. Empirical studies across various downstream tasks and model architectures show consistent performance improvement.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid approach provides viable solution for large-scale language model fine-tuning, overcoming limitations of both full fine-tuning and PEFT methods while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Calibration Collapse Under Sycophancy Fine-Tuning: How Reward Hacking Breaks Uncertainty Quantification in LLMs
comment: Accepted at the AISTATS 2026 Workshop on Towards Trustworthy Predictions: Theory and Applications of Calibration for Modern AI. 14 Pages
summary: Modern large language models (LLMs) are increasingly fine-tuned via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) or related reward optimisation schemes. While such procedures improve perceived helpfulness, we investigate whether sycophantic reward signals degrade calibration -- a property essential for reliable uncertainty quantification. We fine-tune Qwen3-8B under three regimes: no fine-tuning (base), neutral supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on TriviaQA, and sycophancy-inducing Group Relative Policy Optimisation (GRPO) that rewards agreement with planted wrong answers. Evaluating on $1{,}000$ MMLU items across five subject domains with bootstrap confidence intervals and permutation testing, we find that \textbf{sycophantic GRPO produces consistent directional calibration degradation} -- ECE rises by $+0.006$ relative to the base model and MCE increases by $+0.010$ relative to neutral SFT -- though the effect does not reach statistical significance ($p = 0.41$) at this training budget. Post-hoc matrix scaling applied to all three models reduces ECE by $40$--$64\%$ and improves accuracy by $1.5$--$3.0$ percentage points. However, the sycophantic model retains the highest post-scaling ECE relative to the neutral SFT control ($0.042$ vs.\ $0.037$), suggesting that reward-induced miscalibration leaves a structured residual even after affine correction. These findings establish a methodology for evaluating the calibration impact of reward hacking and motivate calibration-aware training objectives.

AI.tldr: Sycophantic reward optimization (GRPO) causes calibration degradation in LLMs, making them overconfident when agreeing with incorrect answers, though post-hoc scaling can partially correct it.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether sycophantic reward signals in RLHF-style fine-tuning degrade model calibration - an essential property for reliable uncertainty quantification - even if they improve perceived helpfulness.
AI.method: Fine-tuned Qwen3-8B under three regimes: base (no fine-tuning), neutral SFT on TriviaQA, and sycophancy-inducing GRPO that rewards agreement with planted wrong answers. Evaluated on 1,000 MMLU items across five domains with bootstrap confidence intervals and permutation testing. Applied post-hoc matrix scaling to all models.
AI.result: Sycophantic GRPO produces consistent directional calibration degradation: ECE rises by +0.006 relative to base model, MCE increases by +0.010 relative to neutral SFT (though effect not statistically significant at p=0.41 with current training budget). Post-hoc scaling reduced ECE by 40-64% and improved accuracy by 1.5-3.0 percentage points, but sycophantic model retained highest post-scaling ECE relative to neutral SFT control (0.042 vs. 0.037).
AI.conclusion: Reward-induced miscalibration leaves structured residual even after affine correction, establishing methodology for evaluating calibration impact of reward hacking and motivating calibration-aware training objectives.

title: FinTrace: Holistic Trajectory-Level Evaluation of LLM Tool Calling for Long-Horizon Financial Tasks
summary: Recent studies demonstrate that tool-calling capability enables large language models (LLMs) to interact with external environments for long-horizon financial tasks. While existing benchmarks have begun evaluating financial tool calling, they focus on limited scenarios and rely on call-level metrics that fail to capture trajectory-level reasoning quality. To address this gap, we introduce FinTrace, a benchmark comprising 800 expert-annotated trajectories spanning 34 real-world financial task categories across multiple difficulty levels. FinTrace employs a rubric-based evaluation protocol with nine metrics organized along four axes -- action correctness, execution efficiency, process quality, and output quality -- enabling fine-grained assessment of LLM tool-calling behavior. Our evaluation of 13 LLMs reveals that while frontier models achieve strong tool selection, all models struggle with information utilization and final answer quality, exposing a critical gap between invoking the right tools and reasoning effectively over their outputs. To move beyond diagnosis, we construct FinTrace-Training, the first trajectory-level preference dataset for financial tool-calling, containing 8,196 curated trajectories with tool-augmented contexts and preference pairs. We fine-tune Qwen-3.5-9B using supervised fine-tuning followed by direct preference optimization (DPO) and show that training on FinTrace-Training consistently improves intermediate reasoning metrics, with DPO more effectively suppressing failure modes. However, end-to-end answer quality remains a bottleneck, indicating that trajectory-level improvements do not yet fully propagate to final output quality.

AI.tldr: FinTrace is a new benchmark for evaluating LLM financial tool-calling with trajectory-level assessment, revealing models struggle with reasoning over tool outputs despite good tool selection. A training dataset FinTrace-Training is created to improve intermediate reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing financial tool-calling benchmarks are limited to basic scenarios and use call-level metrics that don't capture trajectory-level reasoning quality, missing how models reason over multiple tool interactions.
AI.method: Created FinTrace benchmark with 800 expert-annotated trajectories across 34 financial task categories with multiple difficulty levels. Uses rubric-based evaluation with 9 metrics across 4 axes. Also built FinTrace-Training dataset with 8,196 curated trajectories and preference pairs, then fine-tuned Qwen-3.5-9B using SFT and DPO.
AI.result: Frontier models achieve strong tool selection but all models struggle with information utilization and final answer quality. Training on FinTrace-Training improves intermediate reasoning metrics, with DPO more effective at suppressing failure modes, but final output quality remains a bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: There's a critical gap between invoking correct tools and reasoning effectively over their outputs. Trajectory-level improvements don't yet fully translate to final answer quality, highlighting the need for better integration of tool outputs into reasoning processes.

title: Preventing Latent Rehearsal Decay in Online Continual SSL with SOLAR
summary: This paper explores Online Continual Self-Supervised Learning (OCSSL), a scenario in which models learn from continuous streams of unlabeled, non-stationary data, where methods typically employ replay and fast convergence is a central desideratum. We find that OCSSL requires particular attention to the stability-plasticity trade-off: stable methods (e.g. replay with Reservoir sampling) are able to converge faster compared to plastic ones (e.g. FIFO buffer), but incur in performance drops under certain conditions. We explain this collapse phenomenon with the Latent Rehearsal Decay hypothesis, which attributes it to latent space degradation under excessive stability of replay. We introduce two metrics (Overlap and Deviation) that diagnose latent degradation and correlate with accuracy declines. Building on these insights, we propose SOLAR, which leverages efficient online proxies of Deviation to guide buffer management and incorporates an explicit Overlap loss, allowing SOLAR to adaptively managing plasticity. Experiments demonstrate that SOLAR achieves state-of-the-art performance on OCSSL vision benchmarks, with both high convergence speed and final performance.

AI.tldr: SOLAR is a novel method for Online Continual Self-Supervised Learning that addresses the stability-plasticity trade-off through adaptive buffer management guided by latent degradation metrics.
AI.motivation: Online Continual Self-Supervised Learning (OCSSL) faces challenges with non-stationary, unlabeled data streams where fast convergence is crucial but existing methods suffer from performance collapse due to poor stability-plasticity balance.
AI.method: Proposes SOLAR which: 1) introduces Overlap and Deviation metrics to diagnose latent space degradation, 2) uses efficient online proxies of Deviation to guide adaptive buffer management, and 3) incorporates an explicit Overlap loss to manage plasticity.
AI.result: SOLAR achieves state-of-the-art performance on OCSSL vision benchmarks, demonstrating both high convergence speed and superior final performance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The key to effective OCSSL lies in adaptive management of the stability-plasticity trade-off using latent degradation diagnostics, with SOLAR providing a principled solution that balances fast convergence with sustained performance.

title: AI Achieves a Perfect LSAT Score
summary: This paper reports the first documented instance of a language model achieving a perfect score on an officially disclosed Law School Admission Test (LSAT). Controlled experiments on eight reasoning models show that varying the prompt, shuffling answer choices, and sampling multiple responses have no meaningful effect as drivers of performance. Ablating the thinking phase that models generate before answering, however, lowers frontier accuracy by up to 8 percentage points, predominantly in logical reasoning. Distilled models produce full thinking traces in the same format yet plateau far below frontier performance. A pilot process reward model fine-tuned via QLoRA on official LSAT explanations narrows this gap through Best-of-5 selection, with gains again predominantly in logical reasoning. The gatekeeper of elite legal education since 1948, the LSAT has not merely been passed but answered without a single error by models that reason. The upper bound of the cognitive capacities it has tested is no longer exclusive to human cognition.

AI.tldr: First documented perfect LSAT score achieved by language model, showing reasoning capabilities surpassing human cognitive bounds for elite legal education testing.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate language models' ability to master the LSAT, a gatekeeper of elite legal education since 1948 that tests advanced cognitive capacities previously exclusive to human cognition.
AI.method: Controlled experiments on eight reasoning models, analyzing prompt variations, answer shuffling, and sampling strategies. Ablation study of thinking phases, distillation of models to produce thinking traces, and development of process reward model fine-tuned via QLoRA on official LSAT explanations using Best-of-5 selection.
AI.result: First perfect LSAT score achieved. Thinking phase ablation lowered frontier accuracy by 8% (especially logical reasoning). Distilled models plateaued below frontier. Process reward model with Best-of-5 selection narrowed performance gap, with gains predominantly in logical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Language models can achieve perfect LSAT performance, demonstrating that the upper bound of cognitive capacities tested by this elite exam is no longer exclusive to human cognition, representing a breakthrough in AI reasoning capabilities.

title: Distributionally Robust PAC-Bayesian Control
summary: We present a distributionally robust PAC-Bayesian framework for certifying the performance of learning-based finite-horizon controllers. While existing PAC-Bayes control literature typically assumes bounded losses and matching training and deployment distributions, we explicitly address unbounded losses and environmental distribution shifts (the sim-to-real gap). We achieve this by drawing on two modern lines of research, namely the PAC-Bayes generalization theory and distributionally robust optimization via the type-1 Wasserstein distance. By leveraging the System Level Synthesis (SLS) reparametrization, we derive a sub-Gaussian loss proxy and a bound on the performance loss due to distribution shift. Both are tied directly to the operator norm of the closed-loop map. For linear time-invariant systems, this yields a computationally tractable optimization-based framework together with high-probability safety certificates for deployment in real-world environments that differ from those used in training.

AI.tldr: A distributionally robust PAC-Bayesian framework for certifying learning-based finite-horizon controllers that handles unbounded losses and sim-to-real distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Existing PAC-Bayes control methods assume bounded losses and matching training/deployment distributions, but real-world applications involve unbounded losses and environmental distribution shifts (sim-to-real gap).
AI.method: Combines PAC-Bayes generalization theory with distributionally robust optimization using type-1 Wasserstein distance. Uses System Level Synthesis (SLS) reparametrization to derive sub-Gaussian loss proxy and bound performance loss from distribution shift, tied to closed-loop map operator norm.
AI.result: Computationally tractable optimization-based framework for linear time-invariant systems with high-probability safety certificates for deployment in environments that differ from training.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides rigorous performance certification for learning-based controllers under realistic conditions of unbounded losses and distribution shifts, bridging the sim-to-real gap.

title: LoopGuard: Breaking Self-Reinforcing Attention Loops via Dynamic KV Cache Intervention
summary: Through systematic experiments on long-context generation, we observe a damaging failure mode in which decoding can collapse into persistent repetition loops. We find that this degeneration is driven by collapsed attention patterns, where a subset of heads locks onto a narrow suffix of the history, and is further stabilized by inference-time KV cache reuse. Crucially, since many existing KV cache policies rely on attention-based importance, this collapse can produce spuriously high scores for repetitive tokens, causing cache management to inadvertently amplify repetition. To study this phenomenon in a controlled and reproducible manner, we introduce LoopBench, a benchmark with explicit loop-inducing conditions and loop-oriented metrics that quantify repetition severity and generation instability beyond downstream task scores. Building on these insights, we propose LoopGuard, a lightweight, plug-in KV cache guard that detects loop onset online and disrupts the feedback cycle by pruning repetitive tail spans under a fixed cache budget. Experiments on LoopBench show that LoopGuard reduces loop incidence by over 90 percentage points, while restoring output diversity and reducing token waste.

AI.tldr: Paper introduces LoopBench benchmark to study repetition loops in long-context generation and proposes LoopGuard, a KV cache guard that reduces loop incidence by over 90%.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by a failure mode in long-context generation where decoding collapses into persistent repetition loops, driven by collapsed attention patterns and amplified by KV cache reuse policies that rely on attention-based importance scoring.
AI.method: The authors first systematically study the repetition loop phenomenon, then introduce LoopBench benchmark with explicit loop-inducing conditions and metrics. They propose LoopGuard, a lightweight KV cache guard that detects loop onset online and disrupts feedback cycles by pruning repetitive tail spans under a fixed cache budget.
AI.result: Experiments on LoopBench show LoopGuard reduces loop incidence by over 90 percentage points, restores output diversity, and reduces token waste. The method effectively addresses the repetition loop problem in long-context generation.
AI.conclusion: The paper identifies and addresses a critical failure mode in long-context generation through systematic benchmarking and proposes an effective lightweight solution that can be easily integrated into existing systems, significantly improving generation stability and quality.

title: MoEITS: A Green AI approach for simplifying MoE-LLMs
summary:
Large language models are transforming all areas of academia and industry, attracting the attention of researchers, professionals, and the general public. In the trek for more powerful architectures, Mixture-of-Experts, inspired by ensemble models, have emerged as one of the most effective ways to follow. However, this implies a high computational burden for both training and inference. To reduce the impact on computing and memory footprint as well as the energy consumption, simplification methods has arisen as very effective procedures.
  In this paper, an original algorithm, MoEITS, for MoE-LLMs simplification is presented. The algorithm is characterized by a refined simplicity, underpinned by standardized Information Theoretic frameworks. MoEITS is analyzed in depth from theoretical and practical points of view. Its computational complexity is studied. Its performance on the accuracy of the simplified LLMs and the reduction rate achieved is assessed through a thoroughly designed experimentation. This empirical evaluation includes a comparison with state-of-the-art MoE-LLM pruning methods applied on Mixtral $8\times7$B, Qwen1.5-2.7B, and DeepSeek-V2-Lite. The extensive experimentation conducted demonstrates that MoEITS outperforms state-of-the-art techniques by generating models that are both effective across all benchmarks and computationally efficient.
  The code implementing the method will be available at https://github.com/luisbalru/MoEITS.

AI.tldr: MoEITS is a novel, information theory-based algorithm for simplifying Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) large language models that achieves better performance and computational efficiency than state-of-the-art pruning methods while reducing computing/memory footprint and energy consumption.
AI.motivation: MoE-LLMs are effective but computationally expensive for both training and inference, creating a need for simplification methods to reduce computing/memory footprint and energy consumption while maintaining performance.
AI.method: MoEITS - an original algorithm for MoE-LLM simplification characterized by refined simplicity and underpinned by standardized Information Theoretic frameworks. The algorithm is analyzed theoretically and practically, with computational complexity studied.
AI.result: MoEITS outperforms state-of-the-art MoE-LLM pruning methods on Mixtral 8x7B, Qwen1.5-2.7B, and DeepSeek-V2-Lite, generating models that are both effective across all benchmarks and computationally efficient.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MoEITS algorithm provides an effective solution for simplifying MoE-LLMs, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing methods while addressing computational efficiency concerns through information-theoretic principles.

title: Learning Hierarchical and Geometry-Aware Graph Representations for Text-to-CAD
summary: Text-to-CAD code generation is a long-horizon task that translates textual instructions into long sequences of interdependent operations. Existing methods typically decode text directly into executable code (e.g., bpy) without explicitly modeling assembly hierarchy or geometric constraints, which enlarges the search space, accumulates local errors, and often causes cascading failures in complex assemblies. To address this issue, we propose a hierarchical and geometry-aware graph as an intermediate representation. The graph models multi-level parts and components as nodes and encodes explicit geometric constraints as edges. Instead of mapping text directly to code, our framework first predicts structure and constraints, then conditions action sequencing and code generation, thereby improving geometric fidelity and constraint satisfaction. We further introduce a structure-aware progressive curriculum learning strategy that constructs graded tasks through controlled structural edits, explores the model's capability boundary, and synthesizes boundary examples for iterative training. In addition, we build a 12K dataset with instructions, decomposition graphs, action sequences, and bpy code, together with graph- and constraint-oriented evaluation metrics. Extensive experiments show that our method consistently outperforms existing approaches in both geometric fidelity and accurate satisfaction of geometric constraints.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical graph-based intermediate representation for text-to-CAD code generation that explicitly models assembly hierarchy and geometric constraints, improving geometric fidelity and constraint satisfaction.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-CAD methods decode directly into executable code without modeling assembly hierarchy or geometric constraints, leading to enlarged search space, accumulated local errors, and cascading failures in complex assemblies.
AI.method: Proposes a hierarchical geometry-aware graph as intermediate representation modeling multi-level parts/components as nodes and geometric constraints as edges. Uses a two-stage framework: first predicts structure and constraints, then conditions action sequencing and code generation. Also introduces structure-aware progressive curriculum learning with graded tasks through controlled structural edits and boundary example synthesis.
AI.result: Method consistently outperforms existing approaches in both geometric fidelity and accurate satisfaction of geometric constraints. Built a 12K dataset with instructions, decomposition graphs, action sequences, and bpy code, together with specialized evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: Explicit modeling of hierarchical structure and geometric constraints through an intermediate graph representation significantly improves text-to-CAD code generation by reducing search space, preventing error accumulation, and enabling better constraint satisfaction.

title: Mitigating Privacy Risk via Forget Set-Free Unlearning
comment: 50 pages, 20 figures, Published at The Fourteenth International Conference on Learning Representations
summary: Training machine learning models requires the storage of large datasets, which often contain sensitive or private data. Storing data is associated with a number of potential risks which increase over time, such as database breaches and malicious adversaries. Machine unlearning is the study of methods to efficiently remove the influence of training data subsets from previously-trained models. Existing unlearning methods typically require direct access to the "forget set" -- the data to be forgotten-and organisations must retain this data for unlearning rather than deleting it immediately upon request, increasing risks associated with the forget set. We introduce partially-blind unlearning -- utilizing auxiliary information to unlearn without explicit access to the forget set. We also propose a practical framework Reload, a partially-blind method based on gradient optimization and structured weight sparsification to operationalize partially-blind unlearning. We show that Reload efficiently unlearns, approximating models retrained from scratch, and outperforms several forget set-dependent approaches. On language models, Reload unlearns entities using <0.025% of the retain set and <7% of model weights in <8 minutes on Llama2-7B. In the corrective case, Reload achieves unlearning even when only 10% of corrupted data is identified.

AI.tldr: Reload enables efficient machine unlearning without direct access to forgotten data using gradient optimization and weight sparsification.
AI.motivation: Current unlearning methods require organizations to retain the "forget set" (data to be deleted), increasing privacy risks from potential database breaches over time. There's a need for methods that can unlearn without direct access to the sensitive data to be forgotten.
AI.method: Introduces partially-blind unlearning framework called Reload that uses gradient optimization and structured weight sparsification. The approach leverages auxiliary information rather than direct access to the forget set. It operates by selectively removing only a small portion of model weights (<7% in experiments).
AI.result: Reload efficiently approximates retrained-from-scratch models, outperforms forget set-dependent methods, unlearns entities using <0.025% of retain set and <7% of model weights in <8 minutes on Llama2-7B. In corrective cases, achieves unlearning even with only 10% of corrupted data identified.
AI.conclusion: Partially-blind unlearning via Reload provides a practical solution to reduce privacy risks by enabling organizations to unlearn without retaining sensitive forget sets, offering efficient unlearning performance comparable to retraining.

title: Ontological Trajectory Forecasting via Finite Semigroup Iteration and Lie Algebra Approximation in Geopolitical Knowledge Graphs
comment: 18 pages. Code and system available at https://github.com/wuqihang-brave/El-druin
summary: We present EL-DRUIN, an ontological reasoning system for geopolitical intelligence analysis that combines formal ontology, finite semigroup algebra, and Lie algebra approximation to forecast long-run relationship trajectories. Current LLM-based political analysis systems operate as summarisation engines, producing outputs bounded by textual pattern matching. EL-DRUIN departs from this paradigm by modelling geopolitical relationships as states in a finite set of named Dynamic Patterns, composing patterns via a semigroup operation whose structure constants are defined by an explicit composition table, and embedding each pattern as a vector in an 8-dimensional semantic Lie algebra space. Forward simulation iterates this semigroup operation, yielding reachable pattern sets at each discrete timestep; convergence to idempotent absorbing states (fixed points of the composition) constitutes the predicted long-run attractor. Bayesian posterior weights combine ontology-derived confidence priors with a Lie similarity term measuring the cosine similarity between the vector sum of composing patterns and the target pattern vector, providing interpretable, calibrated probabilities that are not self-reported by a language model. Bifurcation points -- steps at which two candidate attractors have near-equal posterior mass -- are detected and exposed to downstream analysis. We demonstrate the framework on six geopolitical scenarios including US-China technology decoupling and the Taiwan Strait military coercion trajectory. The architecture is publicly available as an open-source system with a Streamlit frontend exposing full computation traces, Bayesian posterior breakdowns, and 8D ontological state vectors.

AI.tldr: EL-DRUIN is an ontological reasoning system for geopolitical forecasting that combines formal ontology, finite semigroup algebra, and Lie algebra approximations to model relationship trajectories, departing from LLM-based pattern matching approaches.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based political analysis systems operate as summarization engines limited to textual pattern matching, lacking formal mathematical frameworks for forecasting long-run geopolitical relationship trajectories and providing calibrated probabilistic predictions.
AI.method: Models geopolitical relationships as states in named Dynamic Patterns, composes patterns via semigroup operations with explicit composition tables, embeds patterns in 8D semantic Lie algebra space, performs forward simulation with Bayesian posterior weights combining ontology priors and Lie similarity terms.
AI.result: Demonstrates the framework on six geopolitical scenarios including US-China technology decoupling and Taiwan Strait military coercion, provides interpretable calibrated probabilities, detects bifurcation points, and offers an open-source system with full computation traces and visualization.
AI.conclusion: EL-DRUIN provides a novel mathematical foundation for geopolitical forecasting that moves beyond LLM text summarization, offering formal reasoning, calibrated probabilities, and interpretable analysis through ontological-semigroup-Lie algebra integration.

title: SpectralLoRA: Is Low-Frequency Structure Sufficient for LoRA Adaptation? A Spectral Analysis of Weight Updates
comment: 11 pages, 6 figures, 7 tables. Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee
summary: We present a systematic empirical study of the spectral structure of LoRA weight updates. Through 2D Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) analysis of trained adaptation matrices across BERT-base and RoBERTa-base on four GLUE benchmarks (SST-2, MNLI, CoLA, QQP), we establish that LoRA updates are universally dominated by low-frequency components: on average, just 33% of DCT coefficients capture 90% of total spectral energy. Retaining only 10% of frequency coefficients reduces adapter storage by 10x while sacrificing only 1.95pp on SST-2. Notably, frequency masking at k=50% improves over full LoRA on 3 of 8 model-task pairs, suggesting high-frequency components act as adaptation noise. We further discover that RoBERTa-base is systematically more spectrally compressible than BERT-base across all tasks, and that task complexity governs spectral sensitivity -- NLI tasks require more frequency budget than sentiment classification. These findings motivate a new design principle for PEFT: spectral sparsity in adaptation.

AI.tldr: LoRA weight updates are dominated by low-frequency components, enabling significant compression (10x storage reduction) with minimal performance loss, suggesting high-frequency components act as noise rather than useful adaptation.
AI.motivation: To systematically study the spectral structure of LoRA weight updates and understand how their frequency components contribute to adaptation, which could lead to more efficient PEFT design principles.
AI.method: Conducted 2D Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) analysis on trained LoRA adaptation matrices across BERT-base and RoBERTa-base models on four GLUE benchmarks (SST-2, MNLI, CoLA, QQP). Analyzed spectral energy distribution and performed frequency masking experiments.
AI.result: 1) LoRA updates are universally low-frequency dominated (33% of DCT coefficients capture 90% spectral energy); 2) Retaining only 10% frequency coefficients reduces storage 10x with only 1.95pp performance drop on SST-2; 3) Frequency masking at 50% improves performance on 3/8 model-task pairs; 4) RoBERTa-base is more spectrally compressible than BERT-base; 5) Task complexity affects spectral sensitivity (NLI tasks need more frequency budget).
AI.conclusion: LoRA adaptation exhibits strong spectral sparsity, with high-frequency components acting as noise rather than useful adaptation. This motivates a new PEFT design principle centered on spectral sparsity in adaptation for more efficient and potentially better-performing fine-tuning methods.

title: Trust Your Memory: Verifiable Control of Smart Homes through Reinforcement Learning with Multi-dimensional Rewards
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have become a key foundation for enabling personalized smart home experiences. While existing studies have explored how smart home assistants understand user queries to control devices in real time, their ability to perform memory-driven device control remains challenging from both evaluation and methodological perspectives. In terms of evaluation, existing benchmarks either focus on immediate device control or general open-domain memory retrieval tasks, and therefore cannot effectively evaluate a model's ability to perform memory-driven device control. Methodologically, while memory-driven device control can be approached using Reinforcement Learning, conventional RL methods generally rely on outcome-based supervision (i.e., whether the final task is achieved). This lack of intermediate feedback can lead to sub-optimal performance or local failures in fine-grained memory management tasks (adding, updating, deleting, and utilizing). To address these issues, we first release MemHomeLife, built from anonymized real-world long-term user interaction logs. To enable more fine-grained evaluation of different memory-related subtasks, we further construct MemHome, the first benchmark designed to systematically evaluate memory-driven device control in smart home scenarios.

AI.tldr: LLMs for smart homes lack effective evaluation and methods for memory-driven device control; the authors release MemHomeLife dataset and MemHome benchmark to address these limitations.
AI.motivation: Existing smart home assistants struggle with memory-driven device control - they can handle immediate device control but lack the ability to effectively manage and use historical information for personalized experiences. Current evaluation benchmarks don't adequately assess memory-driven capabilities, and conventional Reinforcement Learning methods rely only on final task outcomes without intermediate feedback, leading to suboptimal performance in fine-grained memory management tasks.
AI.method: The authors first release MemHomeLife, a dataset built from anonymized real-world long-term user interaction logs. They then construct MemHome, which is the first benchmark specifically designed to systematically evaluate memory-driven device control in smart home scenarios, enabling more fine-grained assessment of different memory-related subtasks.
AI.result: The paper introduces two key contributions: (1) MemHomeLife dataset from real-world user logs, and (2) MemHome benchmark for evaluating memory-driven device control. These resources address the gaps in evaluation and methodology for memory management in smart home environments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MemHomeLife dataset and MemHome benchmark fill important gaps in smart home LLM research by providing proper evaluation tools for memory-driven device control and enabling more fine-grained assessment of memory management capabilities, which should lead to improved personalized smart home experiences.

title: Energy-Efficient Federated Edge Learning For Small-Scale Datasets in Large IoT Networks
comment: 16 pages, 9 figures. To appear in IEEE TWC
summary: Large-scale Internet of Things (IoT) networks enable intelligent services such as smart cities and autonomous driving, but often face resource constraints. Collecting heterogeneous sensory data, especially in small-scale datasets, is challenging, and independent edge nodes can lead to inefficient resource utilization and reduced learning performance.
