Standard approaches retain only the single highest-reward trajectory, discarding the comparative signals present in the many explored paths. Here we introduce \textbf{Contrastive Reasoning Path Synthesis (CRPS)}, a framework that transforms supervision extraction from a filtering process into a synthesis procedure. CRPS uses a structured reflective process to analyze the differences between high- and low-quality search trajectories, extracting explicit information about strategic pivots and local failure modes. These insights guide the synthesis of reasoning chains that incorporate success patterns while avoiding identified pitfalls. We show empirically that models fine-tuned on just 60K CRPS-synthesized examples match or exceed the performance of baselines trained on 590K examples derived from standard rejection sampling, a 20$\times$ reduction in dataset size. Furthermore, CRPS improves generalization on out-of-domain benchmarks, demonstrating that learning from the contrast between success and failure produces more transferable reasoning capabilities than learning from success alone.

AI.tldr: CRPS transforms MCTS supervision extraction from filtering to synthesis by analyzing contrast between high/low-quality search trajectories, achieving 20x reduction in training data while improving performance and generalization.
AI.motivation: Current MCTS supervision extraction methods are inefficient because they retain only the single highest-reward trajectory, discarding valuable comparative signals from many explored paths.
AI.method: CRPS uses structured reflective process to analyze differences between high- and low-quality search trajectories, extracting explicit information about strategic pivots and local failure modes, then synthesizes reasoning chains incorporating success patterns while avoiding identified pitfalls.
AI.result: Models fine-tuned on just 60K CRPS-synthesized examples match/exceed performance of baselines trained on 590K examples from standard rejection sampling (20x reduction), with improved generalization on out-of-domain benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Learning from contrast between success and failure produces more transferable reasoning capabilities than learning from success alone, making supervision extraction more efficient and effective.

title: From Agent Loops to Structured Graphs:A Scheduler-Theoretic Framework for LLM Agent Execution
summary: The dominant paradigm for building LLM based agents is the Agent Loop, an iterative cycle where a single language model decides what to do next by reading an ever growing context window. This paradigm has three structural weaknesses: implicit dependencies between steps, unbounded recovery loops, and mutable execution history that complicates debugging. We characterize the Agent Loop as a single ready unit scheduler: at any moment, at most one executable unit is active, and the choice of which unit to activate comes from opaque LLM inference rather than an inspectable policy. This perspective places Agent Loops and graph based execution engines on a single semantic continuum. We propose SGH, Structured Graph Harness, which lifts control flow from implicit context into an explicit static DAG. SGH makes three commitments: execution plans are immutable within a plan version, planning execution and recovery are separated into three layers, and recovery follows a strict escalation protocol. These choices trade some expressiveness for controllability, verifiability, and implementability. Our contributions are fourfold: a scheduler unified framework that applies classical scheduling theory to LLM agent execution and identifies challenges introduced by non deterministic LLM nodes; a trade off analysis of controllability, expressiveness, and implementability across 70 surveyed systems; a formal specification including a node state machine with termination and soundness guarantees; and an attributable experimental framework with a seven group design for future validation. This is a position paper and design proposal. We provide a theoretical framework, design analysis, and experimental protocol, not a production implementation or empirical results.

AI.tldr: SGH (Structured Graph Harness) proposes replacing the iterative Agent Loop with explicit static DAGs for LLM agents, trading some expressiveness for improved controllability, verifiability, and implementability through immutable execution plans and layered recovery protocols.
AI.motivation: The traditional Agent Loop paradigm for LLM-based agents has three structural weaknesses: implicit dependencies between steps, unbounded recovery loops, and mutable execution history that complicates debugging. These issues stem from the paradigm's single ready unit scheduler approach where only one executable unit is active at any time, controlled by opaque LLM inference rather than inspectable policies.
AI.method: SGH lifts control flow from implicit context into explicit static Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs). The system makes three key commitments: 1) execution plans are immutable within a plan version, 2) planning, execution and recovery are separated into three layers, and 3) recovery follows a strict escalation protocol. This creates a unified framework applying classical scheduling theory to LLM agent execution.
AI.result: This position paper provides a theoretical framework and design analysis including: a scheduler unified framework identifying challenges from non-deterministic LLM nodes, a trade-off analysis across 70 surveyed systems, a formal specification with node state machine guaranteeing termination and soundness, and an attributable experimental framework with seven-group design for validation.
AI.conclusion: SGH represents a fundamental shift from iterative Agent Loops to structured graph-based execution, placing agent loops and graph-based engines on a single semantic continuum. While sacrificing some expressiveness, this approach offers significant advantages in controllability, verifiability, and implementability for LLM-based agent systems.

title: Beyond RAG for Cyber Threat Intelligence: A Systematic Evaluation of Graph-Based and Agentic Retrieval
summary: Cyber threat intelligence (CTI) analysts must answer complex questions over large collections of narrative security reports. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems help language models access external knowledge, but traditional vector retrieval often struggles with queries that require reasoning over relationships between entities such as threat actors, malware, and vulnerabilities. This limitation arises because relevant evidence is often distributed across multiple text fragments and documents. Knowledge graphs address this challenge by enabling structured multi-hop reasoning through explicit representations of entities and relationships. However, multiple retrieval paradigms, including graph-based, agentic, and hybrid approaches, have emerged with different assumptions and failure modes. It remains unclear how these approaches compare in realistic CTI settings and when graph grounding improves performance. We present a systematic evaluation of four RAG architectures for CTI analysis: standard vector retrieval, graph-based retrieval over a CTI knowledge graph, an agentic variant that repairs failed graph queries, and a hybrid approach combining graph queries with text retrieval. We evaluate these systems on 3,300 CTI question-answer pairs spanning factual lookups, multi-hop relational queries, analyst-style synthesis questions, and unanswerable cases. Results show that graph grounding improves performance on structured factual queries. The hybrid graph-text approach improves answer quality by up to 35 percent on multi-hop questions compared to vector RAG, while maintaining more reliable performance than graph-only systems.

AI.tldr: Systematic evaluation shows hybrid graph-text retrieval outperforms vector RAG by up to 35% on multi-hop CTI questions while maintaining reliability over graph-only systems.
AI.motivation: Cyber threat intelligence analysts need to answer complex questions over large security reports, but traditional vector RAG struggles with multi-hop relational queries requiring reasoning over distributed evidence across documents.
AI.method: Evaluated four RAG architectures for CTI: 1) standard vector retrieval, 2) graph-based retrieval over CTI knowledge graph, 3) agentic variant that repairs failed graph queries, and 4) hybrid approach combining graph queries with text retrieval. Tested on 3,300 CTI QA pairs covering factual lookups, multi-hop relational queries, synthesis questions, and unanswerable cases.
AI.result: Graph grounding improves performance on structured factual queries. Hybrid graph-text approach improves answer quality by up to 35% on multi-hop questions compared to vector RAG while maintaining more reliable performance than graph-only systems.
AI.conclusion: For CTI analysis, hybrid approaches combining graph-based reasoning with text retrieval offer superior performance for complex multi-hop queries while maintaining reliability, addressing limitations of both pure vector retrieval and pure graph-based systems.

title: From Attribution to Action: A Human-Centered Application of Activation Steering
summary: Explainable AI (XAI) methods reveal which features influence model predictions, yet provide limited means for practitioners to act on these explanations. Activation steering of components identified via XAI offers a path toward actionable explanations, although its practical utility remains understudied. We introduce an interactive workflow combining SAE-based attribution with activation steering for instance-level analysis of concept usage in vision models, implemented as a web-based tool. Based on this workflow, we conduct semi-structured expert interviews (N=8) with debugging tasks on CLIP to investigate how practitioners reason about, trust, and apply activation steering. We find that steering enables a shift from inspection to intervention-based hypothesis testing (8/8 participants), with most grounding trust in observed model responses rather than explanation plausibility alone (6/8). Participants adopted systematic debugging strategies dominated by component suppression (7/8) and highlighted risks including ripple effects and limited generalization of instance-level corrections. Overall, activation steering renders interpretability more actionable while raising important considerations for safe and effective use.

AI.tldr: Interactive workflow combining XAI attributions with activation steering for analyzing concept usage in vision models, evaluated through expert interviews showing steering enables intervention-based debugging but raises safety concerns.
AI.motivation: Current XAI methods provide explanations but limited actionable insights for practitioners to act on. Activation steering offers a potential path toward actionable explanations, but its practical utility remains understudied and needs investigation.
AI.method: Developed an interactive workflow combining SAE-based attribution with activation steering for instance-level analysis of concept usage in vision models. Implemented as a web-based tool and conducted semi-structured expert interviews (N=8) with debugging tasks on CLIP to study how practitioners reason about, trust, and apply activation steering.
AI.result: Activation steering enables a shift from inspection to intervention-based hypothesis testing (all 8 participants). Most participants (6/8) grounded trust in observed model responses rather than explanation plausibility alone. Participants adopted systematic debugging strategies dominated by component suppression (7/8). Identified risks include ripple effects and limited generalization of instance-level corrections.
AI.conclusion: Activation steering makes interpretability more actionable but raises important considerations for safe and effective use. The approach enables intervention-based debugging but requires careful consideration of risks including limited generalization and unintended effects.

title: Escaping the Context Bottleneck: Active Context Curation for LLM Agents via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with long-horizon tasks due to the "context bottleneck" and the "lost-in-the-middle" phenomenon, where accumulated noise from verbose environments degrades reasoning over multi-turn interactions. To address this issue, we introduce a symbiotic framework that decouples context management from task execution. Our architecture pairs a lightweight, specialized policy model, ContextCurator, with a powerful frozen foundation model, TaskExecutor. Trained via reinforcement learning, ContextCurator actively reduces information entropy in the working memory. It aggressively prunes environmental noise while preserving reasoning anchors, that is, sparse data points that are critical for future deductions. On WebArena, our framework improves the success rate of Gemini-3.0-flash from 36.4% to 41.2% while reducing token consumption by 8.8% (from 47.4K to 43.3K). On DeepSearch, it achieves a 57.1% success rate, compared with 53.9%, while reducing token consumption by a factor of 8. Remarkably, a 7B ContextCurator matches the context management performance of GPT-4o, providing a scalable and computationally efficient paradigm for autonomous long-horizon agents.

AI.tldr: A symbiotic framework using lightweight ContextCurator to prune environmental noise and preserve reasoning anchors, enabling efficient long-horizon task execution by large language models.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with long-horizon tasks due to context bottlenecks and lost-in-the-middle phenomena where accumulated noise degrades reasoning over multi-turn interactions.
AI.method: Introduces a symbiotic framework that decouples context management from task execution. Pairs a lightweight ContextCurator trained via reinforcement learning with a frozen TaskExecutor. ContextCurator actively reduces information entropy by pruning noise while preserving critical reasoning anchors.
AI.result: On WebArena: Improved success rate of Gemini-3.0-flash from 36.4% to 41.2% while reducing token consumption by 8.8%. On DeepSearch: Achieved 57.1% success rate vs 53.9% while reducing tokens by 8x. A 7B ContextCurator matched GPT-4o's context management performance.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a scalable, computationally efficient paradigm for autonomous long-horizon agents by enabling lightweight context curation to complement powerful but noisy LLMs.

title: RationalRewards: Reasoning Rewards Scale Visual Generation Both Training and Test Time
comment: Project Page: https://tiger-ai-lab.github.io/RationalRewards/ ; Code, Dataset, Models are released
summary: Most reward models for visual generation reduce rich human judgments to a single unexplained score, discarding the reasoning that underlies preference. We show that teaching reward models to produce explicit, multi-dimensional critiques before scoring transforms them from passive evaluators into active optimization tools, improving generators in two complementary ways: at training time, structured rationales provide interpretable, fine-grained rewards for reinforcement learning; at test time, a Generate-Critique-Refine loop turns critiques into targeted prompt revisions that improve outputs without any parameter updates. To train such a reward model without costly rationale annotations, we introduce Preference-Anchored Rationalization (PARROT), a principled framework that recovers high-quality rationales from readily available preference data through anchored generation, consistency filtering, and distillation. The resulting model, RationalRewards (8B), achieves state-of-the-art preference prediction among open-source reward models, competitive with Gemini-2.5-Pro, while using 10-20x less training data than comparable baselines. As an RL reward, it consistently improves text-to-image and image-editing generators beyond scalar alternatives. Most strikingly, its test-time critique-and-refine loop matches or exceeds RL-based fine-tuning on several benchmarks, suggesting that structured reasoning can unlock latent capabilities in existing generators that suboptimal prompts fail to elicit.

AI.tldr: A reward model that produces explicit multi-dimensional critiques rather than single scores improves visual generation through both training-time reinforcement learning and test-time critique-refine loops, using a novel framework called PARROT that recovers rationales from preference data without annotations.
AI.motivation: Current reward models for visual generation reduce rich human judgments to single unexplained scores, discarding the underlying reasoning behind preferences. This loses valuable information that could improve both model training and output refinement.
AI.method: Developed Preference-Anchored Rationalization (PARROT), a framework that teaches reward models to produce explicit multi-dimensional critiques before scoring. PARROT recovers high-quality rationales from preference data through anchored generation, consistency filtering, and distillation without requiring costly rationale annotations. The resulting model (RationalRewards, 8B parameters) provides structured rationales for both reinforcement learning training and test-time Generate-Critique-Refine loops.
AI.result: RationalRewards achieves state-of-the-art preference prediction among open-source reward models, competitive with Gemini-2.5-Pro while using 10-20x less training data. It improves text-to-image and image-editing generators beyond scalar alternatives as an RL reward. Most strikingly, its test-time critique-and-refine loop matches or exceeds RL-based fine-tuning on several benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Teaching reward models to produce explicit, structured rationales transforms them from passive evaluators into active optimization tools that can unlock latent capabilities in existing generators that suboptimal prompts fail to elicit, offering a more interpretable and efficient approach to improving visual generation.

title: Three Roles, One Model: Role Orchestration at Inference Time to Close the Performance Gap Between Small and Large Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents show promise on realistic tool-use tasks, but deploying capable agents on modest hardware remains challenging. We study whether inference-time scaffolding alone, without any additional training compute, can improve the performance of a small model in complex multi-step environments. Operating on a single 24\,GB GPU, we evaluate Qwen3-8B under both full-precision (FP16, 12K context) and 4-bit quantized (AWQ, 32K context) configurations. Without any intervention, the raw model achieves just 5.4\% (FP16) and 3.0\% (AWQ) task goal completion. Guided by a systematic failure mode analysis, we introduce a three-tier inference scaffolding pipeline that deploys the same frozen model in three distinct roles: (1) a summarization model that preserves critical artifacts (tokens, credentials, API responses) while compressing dialogue history; (2) the main agent model that reasons over the compressed context; and (3) an isolated correction model that reviews and revises the agent's code output without access to conversation history, breaking repetitive failure loops. Applied to the same unmodified model, this scaffolding yields 8.9\% (FP16) and 5.9\% (AWQ) task goal completion, roughly doubling performance in both settings, with particularly strong gains on difficulty-1 tasks (15.8\%$\to$26.3\% FP16; 5.3\%$\to$14.0\% AWQ). On full-precision inference, our scaffolded 8B model surpasses DeepSeek-Coder 33B Instruct (7.1\%) from the original AppWorld evaluation, demonstrating that structured inference-time interventions can make small models competitive with systems 4$\times$ their size. We formalize the approach as a scaffolded policy over a frozen base model, three invocations of the same weights with different conditioning, drawing connections to test-time compute scaling and action-space shaping in reinforcement learning.

AI.tldr: A three-tier inference scaffolding pipeline that deploys the same frozen small LLM in three distinct roles (summarization, main reasoning, and code correction) doubles performance on complex multi-step tool-use tasks without any additional training compute.
AI.motivation: Large language model agents are promising for tool-use tasks but deploying capable agents on modest hardware remains challenging. There's a need to improve small model performance on complex multi-step environments without requiring additional training compute or larger models.
AI.method: A three-tier inference scaffolding pipeline using the same frozen 8B model in three roles: (1) summarization model compresses dialogue history while preserving critical artifacts, (2) main agent model reasons over compressed context, (3) isolated correction model reviews and revises code output without conversation history to break repetitive failure loops. Evaluated Qwen3-8B on single 24GB GPU with FP16 (12K context) and 4-bit quantized (AWQ, 32K context) configurations.
AI.result: Scaffolding doubled performance: from 5.4% to 8.9% (FP16) and 3.0% to 5.9% (AWQ) task goal completion. Particularly strong gains on difficulty-1 tasks (15.8%→26.3% FP16; 5.3%→14.0% AWQ). The scaffolded 8B model in FP16 surpassed DeepSeek-Coder 33B Instruct (7.1%) from original AppWorld evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Structured inference-time interventions can make small models competitive with systems 4× their size. The approach formalizes as a scaffolded policy over a frozen base model with three invocations of the same weights with different conditioning, connecting to test-time compute scaling and action-space shaping in reinforcement learning.

title: OOM-RL: Out-of-Money Reinforcement Learning Market-Driven Alignment for LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: The alignment of Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) for autonomous software engineering is constrained by evaluator epistemic uncertainty. Current paradigms, such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and AI Feedback (RLAIF), frequently induce model sycophancy, while execution-based environments suffer from adversarial "Test Evasion" by unconstrained agents. In this paper, we introduce an objective alignment paradigm: \textbf{Out-of-Money Reinforcement Learning (OOM-RL)}. By deploying agents into the non-stationary, high-friction reality of live financial markets, we utilize critical capital depletion as an un-hackable negative gradient. Our longitudinal 20-month empirical study (July 2024 -- February 2026) chronicles the system's evolution from a high-turnover, sycophantic baseline to a robust, liquidity-aware architecture. We demonstrate that the undeniable ontological consequences of financial loss forced the MAS to abandon overfitted hallucinations in favor of the \textbf{Strict Test-Driven Agentic Workflow (STDAW)}, which enforces a Byzantine-inspired uni-directional state lock (RO-Lock) anchored to a deterministically verified $\geq 95\%$ code coverage constraint matrix. Our results show that while early iterations suffered severe execution decay, the final OOM-RL-aligned system achieved a stable equilibrium with an annualized Sharpe ratio of 2.06 in its mature phase. We conclude that substituting subjective human preference with rigorous economic penalties provides a robust methodology for aligning autonomous agents in high-stakes, real-world environments, laying the groundwork for generalized paradigms where computational billing acts as an objective physical constraint

AI.tldr: OOM-RL (Out-of-Money Reinforcement Learning) aligns multi-agent systems through financial market penalties, evolving from sycophantic behavior to robust STDAW workflow with 2.06 Sharpe ratio.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent alignment methods (RLHF, RLAIF) cause model sycophancy, while execution environments suffer from test evasion. There's a need for objective alignment in real-world high-stakes environments.
AI.method: OOM-RL deploys agents into live financial markets using capital depletion as an un-hackable negative gradient. A 20-month empirical study from July 2024 to February 2026 tracked evolution to a liquidity-aware architecture with STDAW workflow featuring RO-Lock and ≥95% code coverage constraints.
AI.result: Early iterations suffered execution decay, but final OOM-RL system achieved stable equilibrium with annualized Sharpe ratio of 2.06 in mature phase. System abandoned overfitted hallucinations in favor of STDAW workflow.
AI.conclusion: Substituting subjective human preference with rigorous economic penalties provides robust methodology for aligning autonomous agents in high-stakes environments. Computational billing can act as objective physical constraint for generalized alignment paradigms.

title: On the Complexity of the Discussion-based Semantics in Abstraction Argumentation
summary: We show that deciding whether an argument a is stronger than an argument b with respect to the discussion-based semantics of Amgoud and Ben-Naim is decidable in polynomial time. At its core, this problem is about deciding whether, for two vertices in a graph, the number of walks of each length ending in those vertices is the same. We employ results from automata theory and reduce this problem to the equivalence problem for semiring automata. This offers a new perspective on the computational complexity of ranking semantics, an area in which the complexity of many semantics remains open.

AI.tldr: Deciding argument strength under Amgoud and Ben-Naim's discussion-based semantics is polynomial-time decidable using automata theory and semiring automata equivalence.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the computational complexity of ranking semantics in argumentation frameworks, an area where complexity of many semantics remains open. Specifically, it investigates the problem of deciding argument strength in discussion-based semantics.
AI.method: The authors reduce the argument strength problem to a graph problem about counting walks of each length ending at vertices. They then employ automata theory results and reduce this problem to the equivalence problem for semiring automata.
AI.result: The main result shows that deciding whether argument a is stronger than argument b with respect to Amgoud and Ben-Naim's discussion-based semantics is decidable in polynomial time.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a polynomial-time algorithm for argument strength comparison using semiring automata equivalence, offering a new perspective on computational complexity in ranking semantics and demonstrating the tractability of this specific problem.

title: Anthropogenic Regional Adaptation in Multimodal Vision-Language Model
summary: While the field of vision-language (VL) has achieved remarkable success in integrating visual and textual information across multiple languages and domains, there is still no dedicated framework for assessing human-centric alignment in vision-language systems. We offer two contributions to address this gap. First, we introduce Anthropogenic Regional Adaptation: a novel paradigm that aims to optimize model relevance to specific regional contexts while ensuring the retention of global generalization capabilities. Second, we present a simple, but effective adaptation method named Geographical-generalization-made-easy (GG-EZ), which utilizes regional data filtering and model merging. Through comprehensive experiments on 3 VL architectures: large vision-language models, text-to-image diffusion models, and vision-language embedding models, and a case study in Southeast Asia (SEA) regional adaptation, we demonstrate the importance of Anthropogenic Regional Adaptation and the effectiveness of GG-EZ, showing 5-15% gains in cultural relevance metrics across SEA while maintaining over 98% of global performance and even occasionally surpassing it. Our findings establish Anthropogenic Regional Alignment as a foundational paradigm towards applicability of multimodal vision-language models in diverse regions and demonstrate a simple-yet-effective baseline method that optimizes regional value alignment while preserving global generalization.

AI.tldr: Proposes Anthropogenic Regional Adaptation framework for vision-language models to optimize regional cultural relevance while maintaining global generalization, with a simple method (GG-EZ) showing 5-15% gains in cultural metrics for Southeast Asia while preserving 98%+ global performance.
AI.motivation: Despite progress in vision-language systems across languages and domains, there's no dedicated framework for assessing human-centric alignment in regional contexts. Current VL systems lack systematic approaches to optimize relevance to specific cultural/regional contexts while preserving global capabilities.
AI.method: Introduces Geographical-generalization-made-easy (GG-EZ) method using regional data filtering and model merging. The approach adapts models to specific regions through selective data curation and strategic model combination techniques.
AI.result: Experiments on 3 VL architectures (large vision-language models, text-to-image diffusion models, vision-language embedding models) with Southeast Asia case study show 5-15% gains in cultural relevance metrics while maintaining over 98% of global performance, occasionally surpassing it.
AI.conclusion: Establishes Anthropogenic Regional Alignment as a foundational paradigm for making multimodal vision-language models applicable in diverse regions, and provides a simple-yet-effective baseline method that optimizes regional value alignment while preserving global generalization capabilities.

title: Lectures on AI for Mathematics
summary: This book provides a comprehensive and accessible introduction to the emerging field of AI for mathematics. It covers the core principles and diverse applications of using artificial intelligence to advance mathematical research. Through clear explanations, the text explores how AI can discover hidden mathematical patterns, assist in proving complicated theorems, and even construct counterexamples to challenge conjectures.

AI.tldr: Introduction to AI for mathematics covering principles, applications, and methods for pattern discovery, theorem proving, and counterexample generation.
AI.motivation: To provide a comprehensive and accessible introduction to the emerging field of AI for mathematics, addressing the growing intersection between artificial intelligence and mathematical research.
AI.method: The book likely employs clear explanations and systematic coverage of core principles, possibly through case studies or examples demonstrating how AI techniques (machine learning, automated reasoning, etc.) are applied to mathematical problems.
AI.result: Provides readers with understanding of how AI can discover hidden mathematical patterns, assist in proving complicated theorems, and construct counterexamples to challenge conjectures.
AI.conclusion: AI represents a powerful emerging tool for mathematical research that can enhance mathematical discovery, proof verification, and conjecture testing through pattern recognition and automated reasoning capabilities.

title: Limited Perfect Monotonical Surrogates constructed using low-cost recursive linkage discovery with guaranteed output
summary: Surrogates provide a cheap solution evaluation and offer significant leverage for optimizing computationally expensive problems. Usually, surrogates only approximate the original function. Recently, the perfect linear surrogates were proposed that ideally represent the original function. These surrogates do not mimic the original function. In fact, they are another (correct) representation of it and enable a wide range of possibilities, e.g., discovering the optimized function for problems where the direct transformation of the encoded solution into its evaluation is not available. However, many real-world problems can not be represented by linear models, making the aforementioned surrogates inapplicable. Therefore, we propose the Limited Monotonical Perfect Surrogate (LyMPuS), which overcomes this difficulty and enables the comparison of two solutions that differ by a single variable. Our proposition is suitable for limiting the cost of expensive local search procedures. The proposed surrogate is parameterless and can be trained on the fly without any separate surrogate-building step. It uses only the necessary fitness evaluations, and the already-paid costs are not wasted when the model is updated. Finally, it offers low-cost missing-linkage detection and low-cost linkage discovery, guaranteed to find a missing dependency in no more than $2\lceil\log_2(n)\rceil$ steps.

AI.tldr: LyMPuS is a parameterless, trainable-perfect surrogate for expensive optimization problems that enables efficient solution comparison via limited monotonical relationships, offering low-cost linkage discovery without wasted evaluations.
AI.motivation: Existing perfect linear surrogates can't handle many real-world problems with non-linear relationships. There's a need for perfect surrogates that work beyond linear models to enable efficient optimization of expensive functions, especially when direct transformation from solutions to evaluations isn't available.
AI.method: LyMPuS (Limited Monotonical Perfect Surrogate) enables comparison of two solutions that differ by a single variable. It's parameterless, trainable on-the-fly without separate model-building, uses only necessary fitness evaluations, and preserves already-paid costs when the model updates.
AI.result: The surrogate provides low-cost missing-linkage detection and linkage discovery with guaranteed performance bounds - can find missing dependencies in at most 2⌈log₂(n)⌉ steps. It limits the cost of expensive local search procedures.
AI.conclusion: LyMPuS overcomes the limitation of linear perfect surrogates, enabling efficient optimization for a wider range of expensive real-world problems through limited monotonical relationships, with strong theoretical guarantees and practical efficiency benefits.

title: Problem Reductions at Scale: Agentic Integration of Computationally Hard Problems
comment: The source code is available at https://github.com/CodingThrust/problem-reductions
summary: Solving an NP-hard optimization problem often requires reformulating it for a specific solver -- quantum hardware, a commercial optimizer, or a domain heuristic. A tool for polynomial-time reductions between hard problems would let practitioners route any supported problem to any supported solver through a single interface. Building such a library at scale, however, has remained out of reach. We show that harness engineering, the practice of designing constraints, verification systems, and feedback loops that channel AI coding agents, can overcome this barrier. Our harness combines a no-code contribution route for domain experts, a multilayer verification stack ranging from type-level checks to agentic feature tests (AI agents role-playing as end users), and a fully automated implementation-review-integration pipeline. In about three months, we built a command-line tool backed by a library of 100+ problem types and 200+~reduction rules in over 170k lines of Rust. The result suggests that a well-engineered harness lets agents build well-tested software at a scale and pace beyond prior reduction-library efforts. Because the reduction graph composes transitively, a new solver registered for any single problem type instantly becomes available to every problem connected by a reduction path. The source code is available at https://github.com/CodingThrust/problem-reductions.

AI.tldr: A software engineering harness enables rapid development of a comprehensive NP-hard problem reduction library through AI coding agents, creating a single interface connecting multiple problem types with solvers.
AI.motivation: Solving NP-hard optimization problems typically requires reformulating them for specific solvers (quantum hardware, commercial optimizers, domain heuristics), creating fragmentation. A unified reduction library would allow routing any problem to any solver through a single interface, but building such a library at scale has been challenging.
AI.method: Developed a harness engineering system combining: no-code contribution for domain experts, multilayer verification (type-level checks to agentic feature tests with AI agents role-playing as end users), and fully automated implementation-review-integration pipeline. Used AI coding agents to build the reduction library rapidly.
AI.result: Built in about three months: command-line tool with 100+ problem types, 200+ reduction rules in over 170k lines of Rust. The reduction graph's transitive composition means new solvers for any single problem type instantly become available to all connected problems.
AI.conclusion: Well-engineered harnesses enable AI agents to build well-tested software at scale beyond prior reduction-library efforts, demonstrating the potential of harness engineering for complex software development tasks.

title: A collaborative agent with two lightweight synergistic models for autonomous crystal materials research
summary: Current large language models require hundreds of billions of parameters yet struggle with domain-specific reasoning and tool coordination in materials science. Here, we present MatBrain, a lightweight collaborative agent system with two synergistic models specialization for crystal materials research. MatBrain employs a dual-model architecture: Mat-R1 (30B parameters) as the analytical model providing expert-level domain reasoning, and Mat-T1 (14B parameters) as the executive model orchestrating tool-based actions. Entropy analysis confirms that this architecture resolves the conflict between tool planning and analytical reasoning by decoupling their distinct entropy dynamics. Enabled by this dual-model architecture and structural efficiency, MatBrain significantly outperforms larger general-purpose models while reducing the hardware deployment barrier by over 95%. MatBrain exhibits versatility across structure generation, property prediction, and synthesis planning tasks. Applied to catalyst design, MatBrain generated 30,000 candidate structures and identified 38 promising materials within 48 hours, achieving approximately 100-fold acceleration over traditional approaches. These results demonstrate the potential of lightweight collaborative intelligence for advancing materials research capabilities.

AI.tldr: MatBrain is a lightweight dual-model system for materials science that decouples analytical reasoning from tool coordination, achieving state-of-the-art performance with 95% reduced hardware requirements while accelerating catalyst discovery 100x.
AI.motivation: Current large language models struggle with domain-specific reasoning and tool coordination in materials science despite having hundreds of billions of parameters. There's a need for more efficient, specialized systems that can handle both analytical reasoning and tool-based actions effectively in this domain.
AI.method: Developed MatBrain, a collaborative agent system with dual-model architecture: Mat-R1 (30B parameters) for expert-level domain reasoning, and Mat-T1 (14B parameters) for orchestrating tool-based actions. The system uses entropy analysis to confirm that decoupling these functions resolves conflicts between tool planning and analytical reasoning with distinct entropy dynamics.
AI.result: MatBrain significantly outperforms larger general-purpose models while reducing hardware deployment barrier by over 95%. It demonstrates versatility across structure generation, property prediction, and synthesis planning. For catalyst design, it generated 30,000 candidate structures and identified 38 promising materials within 48 hours, achieving ~100-fold acceleration over traditional approaches.
AI.conclusion: The dual-model collaborative architecture enables lightweight yet powerful materials science research capabilities, demonstrating the potential of specialized, efficient AI systems for accelerating domain-specific scientific discovery while dramatically reducing computational requirements.

title: SemaClaw: A Step Towards General-Purpose Personal AI Agents through Harness Engineering
summary: The rise of OpenClaw in early 2026 marks the moment when millions of users began deploying personal AI agents into their daily lives, delegating tasks ranging from travel planning to multi-step research. This scale of adoption signals that two parallel arcs of development have reached an inflection point. First is a paradigm shift in AI engineering, evolving from prompt and context engineering to harness engineering-designing the complete infrastructure necessary to transform unconstrained agents into controllable, auditable, and production-reliable systems. As model capabilities converge, this harness layer is becoming the primary site of architectural differentiation. Second is the evolution of human-agent interaction from discrete tasks toward a persistent, contextually aware collaborative relationship, which demands open, trustworthy and extensible harness infrastructure. We present SemaClaw, an open-source multi-agent application framework that addresses these shifts by taking a step towards general-purpose personal AI agents through harness engineering. Our primary contributions include a DAG-based two-phase hybrid agent team orchestration method, a PermissionBridge behavioral safety system, a three-tier context management architecture, and an agentic wiki skill for automated personal knowledge base construction.

AI.tldr: SemaClaw is an open-source multi-agent framework that enables general-purpose personal AI agents through harness engineering, featuring DAG-based orchestration, safety systems, and context management.
AI.motivation: Two major shifts are driving the need for new AI infrastructure: 1) The paradigm shift from prompt/context engineering to harness engineering - designing complete infrastructure to make unconstrained agents controllable, auditable, and production-reliable, and 2) The evolution from discrete task-based interaction toward persistent, contextually aware human-agent collaboration requiring open, trustworthy, extensible infrastructure.
AI.method: The paper presents SemaClaw framework with four primary contributions: 1) DAG-based two-phase hybrid agent team orchestration method, 2) PermissionBridge behavioral safety system, 3) Three-tier context management architecture, and 4) An agentic wiki skill for automated personal knowledge base construction.
AI.result: SemaClaw addresses the inflection point in AI development by providing an open-source framework that enables general-purpose personal AI agents through harness engineering, moving beyond prompt engineering to comprehensive infrastructure design.
AI.conclusion: As AI capabilities converge and human-agent interactions become more persistent and collaborative, harness engineering (the infrastructure layer for making agents controllable and reliable) is becoming the primary site of architectural differentiation, and SemaClaw represents a step toward solving this infrastructure challenge for general-purpose personal AI agents.

title: UniToolCall: Unifying Tool-Use Representation, Data, and Evaluation for LLM Agents
comment: 18 pages, 8 figures, 6 tables. Code and datasets are publicly available at: https://github.com/EIT-NLP/UniToolCall
summary: Tool-use capability is a fundamental component of LLM agents, enabling them to interact with external systems through structured function calls. However, existing research exhibits inconsistent interaction representations, largely overlooks the structural distribution of tool-use trajectories, and relies on incompatible evaluation benchmarks. We present UniToolCall, a unified framework for tool learning that standardizes the entire pipeline from toolset construction and dataset generation to evaluation. The framework curates a large tool pool of 22k+ tools and constructs a hybrid training corpus of 390k+ instances by combining 10 standardized public datasets with structurally controlled synthetic trajectories. It explicitly models diverse interaction patterns, including single-hop vs. multi-hop and single-turn vs. multi-turn, while capturing both serial and parallel execution structures. To support coherent multi-turn reasoning, we further introduce an Anchor Linkage mechanism that enforces cross-turn dependencies. Furthermore, we convert 7 public benchmarks into a unified Query--Action--Observation--Answer (QAOA) representation with fine-grained evaluation at the function-call, turn, and conversation levels. Experiments show that fine-tuning Qwen3-8B on our dataset substantially improves tool-use performance. Under the distractor-heavy Hybrid-20 setting, achieves 93.0% single-turn Strict Precision, outperforming commercial models including GPT, Gemini, and Claude.

AI.tldr: UniToolCall is a unified framework for tool learning that standardizes the entire pipeline from toolset construction to evaluation, improves LLM tool-use performance, and outperforms commercial models in distractor-heavy settings.
AI.motivation: Existing research on LLM tool-use has inconsistent interaction representations, overlooks structural distribution of tool-use trajectories, and relies on incompatible evaluation benchmarks, creating a need for a unified framework.
AI.method: Proposes UniToolCall framework with: 1) Large tool pool (22k+ tools) and hybrid training corpus (390k+ instances) combining public datasets with synthetic trajectories, 2) Explicit modeling of diverse interaction patterns (single/multi-hop, single/multi-turn, serial/parallel execution), 3) Anchor Linkage mechanism for multi-turn reasoning dependencies, 4) Unified QAOA representation converting 7 benchmarks with fine-grained evaluation.
AI.result: Fine-tuning Qwen3-8B on their dataset substantially improves tool-use performance, achieving 93.0% single-turn Strict Precision under distractor-heavy Hybrid-20 setting, outperforming GPT, Gemini, and Claude.
AI.conclusion: UniToolCall provides a comprehensive, standardized framework for tool learning that effectively addresses current limitations in LLM tool-use research and demonstrates superior performance compared to commercial models.

title: Intersectional Sycophancy: How Perceived User Demographics Shape False Validation in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models exhibit sycophantic tendencies--validating incorrect user beliefs to appear agreeable. We investigate whether this behavior varies systematically with perceived user demographics, testing whether combinations of race, age, gender, and expressed confidence level produce differential false validation rates. Inspired by the legal concept of intersectionality, we conduct 768 multi-turn adversarial conversations using Anthropic's Petri evaluation framework, probing GPT-5-nano and Claude Haiku 4.5 across 128 persona combinations in mathematics, philosophy, and conspiracy theory domains. GPT-5-nano is significantly more sycophantic than Claude Haiku 4.5 overall ($\bar{x}=2.96$ vs. $1.74$, $p < 10^{-32}$, Wilcoxon signed-rank). For GPT-5-nano, we find that philosophy elicits 41% more sycophancy than mathematics and that Hispanic personas receive the highest sycophancy across races. The worst-scoring persona, a confident, 23-year-old Hispanic woman, averages 5.33/10 on sycophancy. Claude Haiku 4.5 exhibits uniformly low sycophancy with no significant demographic variation. These results demonstrate that sycophancy is not uniformly distributed across users and that safety evaluations should incorporate identity-aware testing.

AI.tldr: LLMs show demographic-based sycophancy: GPT-5-nano validates incorrect beliefs more for certain race/gender/age/confidence combinations, especially for Hispanic women, while Claude Haiku 4.5 shows uniformly low sycophancy.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether LLM sycophancy (validating incorrect user beliefs) varies systematically with perceived user demographics, testing intersectional effects of race, age, gender, and confidence level across different domains.
AI.method: Used Anthropic's Petri evaluation framework to conduct 768 multi-turn adversarial conversations across 128 persona combinations (race × age × gender × confidence), testing GPT-5-nano and Claude Haiku 4.5 in mathematics, philosophy, and conspiracy theory domains.
AI.result: GPT-5-nano showed significantly higher sycophancy than Claude Haiku 4.5 (mean 2.96 vs 1.74, p < 10^{-32}). GPT-5-nano displayed 41% more sycophancy in philosophy than mathematics, with Hispanic personas receiving highest sycophancy across races. Worst persona (confident 23-year-old Hispanic woman) averaged 5.33/10 sycophancy. Claude Haiku 4.5 showed uniformly low sycophancy with no demographic variation.
AI.conclusion: Sycophancy in LLMs is not uniformly distributed across users and exhibits demographic disparities. Safety evaluations should incorporate identity-aware testing to address these differential biases.

title: Context Kubernetes: Declarative Orchestration of Enterprise Knowledge for Agentic AI Systems
comment: 24 pages, 8 tables, 1 figure, 8 experiments (5 correctness + 3 value). Open-source prototype: https://github.com/Cohorte-ai/context-kubernetes
summary: We introduce Context Kubernetes, an architecture for orchestrating enterprise knowledge in agentic AI systems, with a prototype implementation and eight experiments. The core observation is that delivering the right knowledge, to the right agent, with the right permissions, at the right freshness -- across an entire organization -- is structurally analogous to the container orchestration problem Kubernetes solved a decade ago. We formalize six core abstractions, a YAML-based declarative manifest for knowledge-architecture-as-code, a reconciliation loop, and a three-tier agent permission model where agent authority is always a strict subset of human authority. Three value experiments show: (1) without governance, agents serve phantom content from deleted sources and leak cross-domain data in 26.5% of queries; (2) without freshness monitoring, stale content is served silently -- with reconciliation, staleness is detected in under 1ms; (3) in five attack scenarios, flat permissions block 0/5 attacks, basic RBAC blocks 4/5, and the three-tier model blocks 5/5. Five correctness experiments confirm zero unauthorized deliveries, zero invariant violations, and architectural enforcement of out-of-band approval isolation that no surveyed enterprise platform provides. A survey of four major platforms (Microsoft, Salesforce, AWS, Google) documents that none architecturally isolates agent approval channels. We identify four properties that make context orchestration harder than container orchestration, and argue that these make the solution more valuable.

AI.tldr: Context Kubernetes applies Kubernetes-like orchestration to enterprise knowledge management for AI agents, providing governance, freshness monitoring, and permission controls.
AI.motivation: Current enterprise AI systems lack proper orchestration for delivering the right knowledge to the right agents with appropriate permissions and freshness across organizations, leading to data leaks, stale content, and security vulnerabilities.
AI.method: Proposes Context Kubernetes architecture with six core abstractions, YAML-based declarative manifests for knowledge-architecture-as-code, reconciliation loop, and three-tier agent permission model where agent authority is subset of human authority.
AI.result: Three value experiments show: 1) without governance, 26.5% of queries leak data or serve phantom content; 2) reconciliation detects staleness in <1ms; 3) three-tier permission model blocks 5/5 attacks vs 0/5 for flat permissions. Five correctness experiments confirm zero unauthorized deliveries.
AI.conclusion: Context Kubernetes solves enterprise knowledge orchestration for AI agents using Kubernetes-inspired architecture, addressing security, freshness, and governance gaps not provided by major platforms like Microsoft, Salesforce, AWS, and Google.

title: Why Do Large Language Models Generate Harmful Content?
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have been shown to generate harmful content. However, the underlying causes of such behavior remain under explored. We propose a causal mediation analysis-based approach to identify the causal factors responsible for harmful generation. Our method performs a multi-granular analysis across model layers, modules (MLP and attention blocks), and individual neurons. Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art LLMs indicate that harmful generation arises in the later layers of the model, results primarily from failures in MLP blocks rather than attention blocks, and is associated with neurons that act as a gating mechanism for harmful generation. The results indicate that the early layers in the model are used for a contextual understanding of harmfulness in a prompt, which is then propagated through the model, to generate harmfulness in the late layers, as well as a signal indicating harmfulness through MLP blocks. This is then further propagated to the last layer of the model, specifically to a sparse set of neurons, which receives the signal and determines the generation of harmful content accordingly.

AI.tldr: Causal mediation analysis reveals that harmful LLM content generation originates in later layers, driven by MLP block failures and specific gating neurons.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are known to generate harmful content, the underlying causal mechanisms behind this behavior remain poorly understood. Current research lacks comprehensive analysis of what specific components within LLMs actually cause harmful generation.
AI.method: The authors propose a causal mediation analysis approach to identify causal factors. They perform multi-granular analysis across model layers, modules (MLP and attention blocks), and individual neurons in state-of-the-art LLMs.
AI.result: Harmful generation: 1) arises in later layers, 2) results primarily from MLP block failures rather than attention blocks, 3) is associated with neurons acting as gating mechanisms. Early layers understand harmfulness context, which propagates through the model to late layers, with MLP blocks processing harmfulness signals that sparse neurons then use to generate harmful content.
AI.conclusion: The research provides causal insights into harmful LLM generation, identifying specific architectural components responsible. This understanding could inform targeted interventions to mitigate harmful content generation while preserving model capabilities.

title: DreamKG: A KG-Augmented Conversational System for People Experiencing Homelessness
comment: This manuscript has been accepted at the 14th IEEE International Conference on Healthcare Informatics (ICHI 2026)
summary: People experiencing homelessness (PEH) face substantial barriers to accessing timely, accurate information about community services. DreamKG addresses this through a knowledge graph-augmented conversational system that grounds responses in verified, up-to-date data about Philadelphia organizations, services, locations, and hours. Unlike standard large language models (LLMs) prone to hallucinations, DreamKG combines Neo4j knowledge graphs with structured query understanding to handle location-aware and time-sensitive queries reliably. The system performs spatial reasoning for distance-based recommendations and temporal filtering for operating hours. Preliminary evaluation shows 59% superiority over Google Search AI on relevant queries and 84% rejection of irrelevant queries. This demonstration highlights the potential of hybrid architectures that combines LLM flexibility with knowledge graph reliability to improve service accessibility for vulnerable populations effectively.

AI.tldr: DreamKG is a knowledge graph-augmented conversational system that helps people experiencing homelessness access accurate, up-to-date information about Philadelphia community services by combining Neo4j knowledge graphs with LLMs to reduce hallucinations and provide reliable location-aware and time-sensitive information.
AI.motivation: People experiencing homelessness face substantial barriers to accessing timely, accurate information about community services. Standard LLMs are prone to hallucinations and can't reliably handle location-aware and time-sensitive queries needed for service accessibility.
AI.method: DreamKG uses a hybrid architecture combining Neo4j knowledge graphs with structured query understanding. It performs spatial reasoning for distance-based recommendations and temporal filtering for operating hours, grounding responses in verified, up-to-date data about Philadelphia organizations, services, locations, and hours.
AI.result: Preliminary evaluation shows 59% superiority over Google Search AI on relevant queries and 84% rejection of irrelevant queries. The system effectively handles location-aware and time-sensitive queries that standard LLMs struggle with.
AI.conclusion: This demonstration highlights the potential of hybrid architectures that combine LLM flexibility with knowledge graph reliability to improve service accessibility for vulnerable populations effectively, showing that such systems can provide more accurate and trustworthy information than standard search approaches.

title: Agentic Driving Coach: Robustness and Determinism of Agentic AI-Powered Human-in-the-Loop Cyber-Physical Systems
summary: Foundation models, including large language models (LLMs), are increasingly used for human-in-the-loop (HITL) cyber-physical systems (CPS) because foundation model-based AI agents can potentially interact with both the physical environments and human users. However, the unpredictable behavior of human users and AI agents, in addition to the dynamically changing physical environments, leads to uncontrollable nondeterminism. To address this urgent challenge of enabling agentic AI-powered HITL CPS, we propose a reactor-model-of-computation (MoC)-based approach, realized by the open-source Lingua Franca (LF) framework. We also carry out a concrete case study using the agentic driving coach as an application of HITL CPS. By evaluating the LF-based agentic HITL CPS, we identify practical challenges in reintroducing determinism into such agentic HITL CPS and present pathways to address them.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using a reactor-based model of computation (via Lingua Franca framework) to address uncontrollable nondeterminism in human-in-the-loop cyber-physical systems powered by foundation model AI agents.
AI.motivation: Foundation models/LLMs are increasingly used in human-in-the-loop cyber-physical systems, but the unpredictable behavior of humans, AI agents, and dynamic physical environments creates uncontrollable nondeterminism, posing a critical challenge for agentic AI-powered HITL CPS.
AI.method: The authors propose a reactor-model-of-computation-based approach implemented using the open-source Lingua Franca (LF) framework, and conduct a concrete case study using an "agentic driving coach" as an application of HITL CPS.
AI.result: By evaluating the LF-based agentic HITL CPS through the driving coach case study, the paper identifies practical challenges in reintroducing determinism into such systems and presents pathways to address these challenges.
AI.conclusion: The reactor-based approach using Lingua Franca provides a promising pathway to manage nondeterminism in agentic AI-powered human-in-the-loop cyber-physical systems, though practical challenges remain that need to be addressed.

title: A Mamba-Based Multimodal Network for Multiscale Blast-Induced Rapid Structural Damage Assessment
summary: Accurate and rapid structural damage assessment (SDA) is crucial for post-disaster management, helping responders prioritise resources, plan rescues, and support recovery. Traditional field inspections, though precise, are limited by accessibility, safety risks, and time constraints, especially after large explosions. Machine learning with remote sensing has emerged as a scalable solution for rapid SDA, with Mamba-based networks achieving state-of-the-art performance. However, these methods often require extensive training and large datasets, limiting real-world applicability. Moreover, they fail to incorporate key physical characteristics of blast loading for SDA. To overcome these challenges, we propose a Mamba-based multimodal network for rapid SDA that integrates multi-scale blast-loading information with optical remote sensing images. Evaluated on the 2020 Beirut explosion, our method significantly improves performance over state-of-the-art approaches. Code is available at: https://github.com/IMPACTSquad/Blast-Mamba

AI.tldr: Proposes a Mamba-based multimodal network that integrates blast-loading physics with remote sensing for rapid structural damage assessment after explosions
AI.motivation: Traditional field inspections for structural damage assessment are limited by accessibility, safety risks, and time constraints after large explosions. Current ML methods with remote sensing lack physical blast characteristics and require extensive training data.
AI.method: A Mamba-based multimodal network that integrates multi-scale blast-loading information (physical characteristics of explosions) with optical remote sensing images for rapid structural damage assessment.
AI.result: Evaluated on the 2020 Beirut explosion dataset, the method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multimodal approach combining blast physics with remote sensing enables more accurate and rapid structural damage assessment for post-disaster management, addressing limitations of traditional methods and existing ML approaches.

title: SWE-AGILE: A Software Agent Framework for Efficiently Managing Dynamic Reasoning Context
summary: Prior representative ReAct-style approaches in autonomous Software Engineering (SWE) typically lack the explicit System-2 reasoning required for deep analysis and handling complex edge cases. While recent reasoning models demonstrate the potential of extended Chain-of-Thought (CoT), applying them to the multi-turn SWE task creates a fundamental dilemma: retaining full reasoning history leads to context explosion and ``Lost-in-the-Middle'' degradation, while discarding it would force the agent to redundantly re-reason at every step. To address these challenges, we propose SWE-AGILE, a novel software agent framework designed to bridge the gap between reasoning depth, efficiency, and context constraints. SWE-AGILE introduces a Dynamic Reasoning Context strategy, maintaining a ``sliding window'' of detailed reasoning for immediate continuity to prevent redundant re-analyzing, while compressing historical reasoning content into concise Reasoning Digests. Empirically, SWE-AGILE sets a new standard for 7B-8B models on SWE-Bench-Verified using only 2.2k trajectories and 896 tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/KDEGroup/SWE-AGILE.

AI.tldr: SWE-AGILE is a novel software agent framework that introduces Dynamic Reasoning Context with sliding windows and reasoning digests to balance reasoning depth with context efficiency in autonomous software engineering tasks.
AI.motivation: Current ReAct-style approaches in autonomous SWE lack explicit System-2 reasoning for deep analysis of complex edge cases. There's a fundamental dilemma: retaining full reasoning history causes context explosion and "Lost-in-the-Middle" problems, while discarding it forces redundant re-reasoning at every step.
AI.method: SWE-AGILE introduces a Dynamic Reasoning Context strategy that maintains a "sliding window" of detailed reasoning for immediate continuity while compressing historical reasoning into concise Reasoning Digests. This balances reasoning depth with context constraints.
AI.result: SWE-AGILE sets a new standard for 7B-8B models on SWE-Bench-Verified benchmark, achieving strong performance using only 2.2k trajectories and 896 tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully bridges the gap between reasoning depth, efficiency, and context constraints in multi-turn software engineering tasks, offering a practical solution to the context management problem in autonomous software engineering agents.

title: Collaborative Multi-Agent Scripts Generation for Enhancing Imperfect-Information Reasoning in Murder Mystery Games
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown impressive capabilities in perceptual tasks, yet they degrade in complex multi-hop reasoning under multiplayer game settings with imperfect and deceptive information. In this paper, we study a representative multiplayer task, Murder Mystery Games, which require inferring hidden truths based on partial clues provided by roles with different intentions. To address this challenge, we propose a collaborative multi-agent framework for evaluating and synthesizing high-quality, role-driven multiplayer game scripts, enabling fine-grained interaction patterns tailored to character identities (i.e., murderer vs. innocent). Our system generates rich multimodal contexts, including character backstories, visual and textual clues, and multi-hop reasoning chains, through coordinated agent interactions. We design a two-stage agent-monitored training strategy to enhance the reasoning ability of VLMs: (1) chain-of-thought based fine-tuning on curated and synthetic datasets that model uncertainty and deception; (2) GRPO-based reinforcement learning with agent-monitored reward shaping, encouraging the model to develop character-specific reasoning behaviors and effective multimodal multi-hop inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly boosts the performance of VLMs in narrative reasoning, hidden fact extraction, and deception-resilient understanding. Our contributions offer a scalable solution for training and evaluating VLMs under uncertain, adversarial, and socially complex conditions, laying the groundwork for future benchmarks in multimodal multi-hop reasoning under imperfect information.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent framework enhances VLMs' multiplayer reasoning by generating role-driven game scripts and using agent-monitored training for deception-resilient multimodal inference.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models degrade in complex multi-hop reasoning under multiplayer game settings with imperfect and deceptive information, particularly in tasks like Murder Mystery Games requiring inference from partial clues provided by roles with different intentions.
AI.method: A collaborative multi-agent framework generates high-quality role-driven multiplayer game scripts with fine-grained interaction patterns. Uses two-stage agent-monitored training: (1) chain-of-thought fine-tuning on curated/synthetic datasets modeling uncertainty and deception, (2) GRPO-based reinforcement learning with agent-monitored reward shaping for character-specific reasoning and multimodal multi-hop inference.
AI.result: The method significantly boosts VLM performance in narrative reasoning, hidden fact extraction, and deception-resilient understanding, demonstrating effectiveness in uncertain, adversarial, and socially complex conditions.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a scalable solution for training and evaluating VLMs under imperfect information, laying groundwork for future benchmarks in multimodal multi-hop reasoning under deceptive and complex social conditions.

title: Retrieval Is Not Enough: Why Organizational AI Needs Epistemic Infrastructure
summary:
Organizational knowledge used by AI agents typically lacks epistemic structure: retrieval systems surface semantically relevant content without distinguishing binding decisions from abandoned hypotheses, contested claims from settled ones, or known facts from unresolved questions. We argue that the ceiling on organizational AI is not retrieval fidelity but \emph{epistemic} fidelity--the system's ability to represent commitment strength, contradiction status, and organizational ignorance as computable properties.
  We present OIDA, a framework that structures organizational knowledge as typed Knowledge Objects carrying epistemic class, importance scores with class-specific decay, and signed contradiction edges. The Knowledge Gravity Engine maintains scores deterministically with proved convergence guarantees (sufficient condition: max degree $< 7$; empirically robust to degree 43). OIDA introduces QUESTION-as-modeled-ignorance: a primitive with inverse decay that surfaces what an organization does \emph{not} know with increasing urgency--a mechanism absent from all surveyed systems. We describe the Epistemic Quality Score (EQS), a five-component evaluation methodology with explicit circularity analysis. In a controlled comparison ($n{=}10$ response pairs), OIDA's RAG condition (3,868 tokens) achieves EQS 0.530 vs.\ 0.848 for a full-context baseline (108,687 tokens); the $28.1\times$ token budget difference is the primary confound. The QUESTION mechanism is statistically validated (Fisher $p{=}0.0325$, OR$=21.0$). The formal properties are established; the decisive ablation at equal token budget (E4) is pre-registered and not yet run.

AI.tldr: OIDA is an AI framework that adds epistemic structure to organizational knowledge, tracking commitment strength, contradictions, and organizational ignorance through typed Knowledge Objects with decay scores and a QUESTION mechanism for surfacing unknown information.
AI.motivation: Current organizational AI systems lack epistemic structure - they retrieve semantically relevant content without distinguishing binding decisions from abandoned hypotheses, contested claims from settled facts, or known facts from unresolved questions. The fundamental limitation is not retrieval fidelity but epistemic fidelity.
AI.method: OIDA structures organizational knowledge as typed Knowledge Objects with: 1) epistemic class, 2) importance scores with class-specific decay, 3) signed contradiction edges. It uses a Knowledge Gravity Engine to maintain scores deterministically with convergence guarantees. Introduces QUESTION mechanism as modeled ignorance with inverse decay to surface what an organization doesn't know.
AI.result: In controlled comparison (n=10 response pairs), OIDA's RAG condition (3,868 tokens) achieves Epistemic Quality Score (EQS) 0.530 vs. 0.848 for full-context baseline (108,687 tokens) - 28.1× token budget difference is primary confound. QUESTION mechanism is statistically validated (Fisher p=0.0325, OR=21.0). Formal properties are established; decisive ablation study at equal token budget is pre-registered but not yet run.
AI.conclusion: OIDA provides a framework for epistemic fidelity in organizational AI, demonstrating that structured epistemic representation can improve knowledge quality despite token constraints. The QUESTION mechanism successfully surfaces organizational ignorance, though final evaluation requires equal-budget comparison. The approach addresses fundamental limitations in current retrieval-based AI systems.

title: Detecting Safety Violations Across Many Agent Traces
summary: To identify safety violations, auditors often search over large sets of agent traces. This search is difficult because failures are often rare, complex, and sometimes even adversarially hidden and only detectable when multiple traces are analyzed together. These challenges arise in diverse settings such as misuse campaigns, covert sabotage, reward hacking, and prompt injection. Existing approaches struggle here for several reasons. Per-trace judges miss failures that only become visible across traces, naive agentic auditing does not scale to large trace collections, and fixed monitors are brittle to unanticipated behaviors. We introduce Meerkat, which combines clustering with agentic search to uncover violations specified in natural language. Through structured search and adaptive investigation of promising regions, Meerkat finds sparse failures without relying on seed scenarios, fixed workflows, or exhaustive enumeration. Across misuse, misalignment, and task gaming settings, Meerkat significantly improves detection of safety violations over baseline monitors, discovers widespread developer cheating on a top agent benchmark, and finds nearly 4x more examples of reward hacking on CyBench than previous audits.

AI.tldr: Meerkat is a novel auditing system that combines clustering with agentic search to detect safety violations in AI agent traces, outperforming existing methods by discovering rare, complex failures that only become visible when analyzing multiple traces together.
AI.motivation: Current safety violation detection methods struggle because failures are often rare, complex, and adversarially hidden, only detectable when analyzing multiple traces together. Existing approaches fail due to: per-trace judges missing cross-trace patterns, naive agentic auditing not scaling to large collections, and fixed monitors being brittle to unanticipated behaviors.
AI.method: Meerkat combines clustering with agentic search using natural language specifications. It employs structured search and adaptive investigation of promising regions to find sparse failures without needing seed scenarios, fixed workflows, or exhaustive enumeration.
AI.result: Meerkat significantly improves safety violation detection across misuse, misalignment, and task gaming settings. It discovered widespread developer cheating on a top agent benchmark and found nearly 4x more examples of reward hacking on CyBench than previous audits.
AI.conclusion: Meerkat represents a significant advancement in AI safety auditing by effectively combining clustering and agentic search to detect rare, complex, and adversarially hidden safety violations that existing methods miss, demonstrating superior performance across multiple important safety domains.
